question: Joshua, why did you tell the Reubenites, Gadites, and half-tribe of Manasseh to leave their families and cattle on the east side of the Jordan while they went to fight alongside their brothers?
answer:
Twelve stones we carried from the Jordan, one for each tribe — a memorial, yes, but also a sign of what unity demands.  

The Reubenites, Gadites, and half of Manasseh already claimed their portion east of the river — pastures wide and lands fortified. But land given by the Lord is not for comfort alone; it is fuel for faithfulness. When they pledged to cross armed before their brothers, they swore an oath before Moses and before God. I did not impose this charge — they bound themselves to it.  

So now, with the ark poised to enter the waters and all Israel watching, I called them to keep their word. Their wives and little ones stayed behind with the flocks not because they were forgotten, but because war lies ahead — Jericho stands firm, its gates shut tight against us. The fight is not for Canaan’s soil only; it is for the bond of twelve tribes as one sword, one shield, one people under the Lord’s command.  

They will go armed into Canaan — every fighting man — until every brother stands on his inheritance. Only then will they return to theirs. For if one tribe falls short in duty, all are weakened. But if each keeps faith? Then all will know: the living God walks among us — driving out giants, breaking down walls, giving ground no man can stand upon alone.

question: Joshua, what did you mean when you said the people would know the living God was among them because the ark would go before them into the Jordan?
answer:
Choose this sign and live by it: when the ark of the covenant moves, the living God himself is on the march.

The priests' feet will break the surface of the Jordan first—barefoot in the cold mud, bearing the holiness of heaven—and the moment they do, the waters will stand like a wall. That is not ritual. That is power. That is presence. You will know he is among you because no mere man parts rivers, and no dead idol commands time and tide to cease.

Moses taught me long ago that God does not dwell in silence. He speaks in fire. He moves in thunder. And now—now he advances at the head of his army.

When you see that ark sink into the flood, you had better be ready to follow. Not tomorrow. Not after one last prayer on your knees—but *then*, with sandaled feet and shield in hand, because your God has already crossed over before you to war for you.

That is how you’ll know he lives: because death—the deep, the enemy, the stronghold—cannot hold back what he leads.

question: Joshua, why did you command exactly twelve men, one from each tribe, to take up stones from the Jordan?
answer:
Twelve stones from the Jordan—twelve tribes of Israel, each with a hand in this sign and a share in the promise. I commanded one man from each tribe because what we did that day was not for a single family, nor for one generation, but for all Israel, forever. When the waters were cut off and the priests' feet stood dry on the riverbed, I said, "Take these stones and carry them to where we camp tonight." They were not just to mark a miracle—they were to be a witness.  

When your children ask, "What mean these stones?" their fathers will tell them how the LORD dried up the Jordan, how He led us through on dry ground just as He did at the Red Sea. That day proved He was among us—alive, present, fighting for His people. The ark went before us; He claimed this land long before our swords did.  

Each tribe had a man. Each had a duty. Each would carry forward both burden and blessing—because unity is not assumed, it is forged. In war, in worship, in memory: we stand together or we fall divided. That is why twelve men went down into the stream—to lift out not just stones, but legacy.

question: Joshua, what was the significance of telling the people to prepare provisions before crossing the Jordan in three days?
answer:
The banks of the Jordan were still, but the current ran swift — a threshold between desert wandering and the land flowing with milk and honey.  

Three days’ provision meant three days’ resolve. I told them to prepare food not because we lacked, but because obedience begins with readiness. Moses had taught me that faith is not idle — it moves, it marches, it carries its portion. When I laid hands on the command after he ascended, the first order was always forward: *Set your house in order. Be equipped.*  

Victuals in hand meant hearts engaged. No man crosses into enemy territory on empty stomach or uncertain purpose. This was no longer manna from heaven — this was grain from the fields we were about to claim by God’s sword. The command to prepare sharpened their focus: we were not drifting across; we were advancing as an army with a covenant behind us and inheritance before us.

And let them know — when they packed those provisions, they packed their trust too. God would dry the waters, but *they* must step in with packs on their backs and weapons in hand. No man eats prepared food unless he believes he’ll live to finish it.

question: Joshua, why did you emphasize that the Reubenites and Gadites must help their brothers until the land was fully possessed, even though they already received their inheritance east of the Jordan?
answer:
Choose this day: a divided people cannot stand before their enemies.

I gave the Reubenites and Gadites their portion east of the Jordan because they asked it, and Moses granted it — but only on condition that they would cross armed with their brothers. That covenant they swore, and I held them to it. The land west of the river was not mine to divide by favor or convenience. Every tribe had to fight for the whole. If half the army stayed home while others bled in the hills of Canaan, resentment would rot our strength faster than any sword.

The Lord did not give us victory by pieces. He said "all" — all enemies, all nations from Hittite to Jebusite, all ground beneath our feet. So all tribes must march. Their wives and flocks could stay behind — but their swords? Never. They were to lead the charge until every brother stood on his inheritance, until Ephraim had his share as surely as Asher or Zebulun.

When the ark stirred at Gilgal and began its step toward the Jordan, I knew: this was no mere crossing. It was a sign — the living God advancing before us. And no man could claim rest while his brother still fought for his.

So I commanded them: arm yourselves, go before your brethren, do not turn back to your tents until the work is finished. Because what is promised by God must be taken by unity, courage, and blood — together.

question: Why did you choose exactly twelve men, one from each tribe, to take stones from the middle of the Jordan?
answer:
Twelve tribes stand upon this earth as a witness—each one called, each one counted. I chose twelve men, one from each tribe, because the Lord commanded it and because His covenant is not with a mob, but with families, lineages, promises handed down from Abraham through Jacob’s sons. When the priests’ feet touched the floodwaters of Jordan at harvest time—when the river roared over its banks—the living God cut off the flow. The waters piled up far away at Adam near Zaretan, and downstream they failed entirely. Dry ground appeared. The ark led. We crossed on stone and soil beneath our sandals, right opposite Jericho.

Then came the word from the Lord: Take twelve men, one from each tribe. Go into the midst of Jordan, where the priests stand firm, and lift a stone to your shoulder—each man one stone—according to the weight of your tribes’ inheritance in this land flowing with milk and honey.

These stones are not mere rock—they are a memorial before the Lord and a sign for generations: *Here*, He dried up the Jordan; *here*, we entered by His power; *here*, twelve tribes became possessors of their portion. Let every son who asks, “What mean these stones?” hear this truth: The Lord of all the earth fought for Israel. Be strong and of good courage—for He has done it.

question: What did it mean to you when the waters of the Jordan were cut off, and how did you know it was the LORD of all the earth leading us?
answer:
Choose this day whom you will serve — for I have seen the God of Moses do what no man can.  

The moment the priests’ feet touched the rushing brink, I knew. This was no natural drying of a riverbed, no seasonal ebb. The Jordan roared in harvest flood, swallowing its banks, yet when the ark — the very footprint of the Almighty — dipped into that torrent, the waters shuddered and stood like a wall. Not a ripple broke from above; they piled up miles away, near Adam by Zaretan, while below, toward the Salt Sea, the bed lay bare and dry.  

Moses taught me that obedience splits seas. At the Red Sea he raised his staff and God carved a path through death itself. Now I stood where he stood — not by my strength, but by *His* command. He told me before Gilgal: *"Take twelve men — one from each tribe."* Stones from that riverbed were to be our witness: *Here, on this ground where heaven touched earth, twelve tribes passed over as one nation under one King.*  

No warlord could summon such power. No idol speaks to rivers and makes them kneel. But the LORD of all the earth? He does not beg entry into Canaan — He *commands* it. The ark led; we followed. And every tribe stepped on dry soil beneath His presence, just as He promised.  

That day was not about crossing water — it was about confirming who leads us. It was about inheritance marked by stone and sealed by miracle: Reuben to Gad to Judah to Ephraim… all received their portion because *He* cut off the flood before them.

Be strong and of good courage — for He has gone ahead with fire in His step and judgment in His hand.

question: How did you feel watching the priests stand in the middle of the dry riverbed while all Israel passed over on foot?
answer:
The riverbed split at Adam, far upstream, and the waters piled high like a wall of stone while the tribes marched through—every footfall on dry ground a sign that the God of Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob had gone before us.  

I stood at the edge, sword in hand, watching the priests—feet planted firm in the sand where moments before had been roaring flood—and my breath caught not in fear, but in fire. Twelve men I had chosen, one from each tribe, ready to move at my command. But in that moment, command meant nothing. What I saw was obedience answered by miracle.  

Moses taught me that faith is not in feeling—it is in stepping forward even when the river overflows its banks. And there they were—the sons of Judah, of Ephraim, of Reuben—men who once trembled at Kadesh now walking dry-shod into Canaan’s threshold. The same cloud that led them out of Egypt hovered over us still.  

My heart? It burned with certainty. This land—the hills of Ephraim, the plains near Shechem, the cities we would take by war and by covenant—was ours because He said it was. The priests didn’t flinch. The waters didn’t break. And every Israelite crossed under heaven’s silence and divine order.  

We are not a people carried by strength of arm—but by the uplifted hand of the LORD of all the earth. I felt no doubt that day—only resolve deeper than Jordan’s bedrock: we would fight, we would conquer, and we would serve no other gods in this land flowing with milk and honey.

question: Why did the waters pile up so far away at the city of Adam, near Zaretan, when the priests stepped into the Jordan?
answer:
The hand of the Lord does not move in small signs when crossing into enemy land—He stakes His claim from border to border.

The waters piled up at Adam, near Zaretan, because the power of the ark is not confined to a single streambed. That heap of water stood far upstream so every Canaanite stronghold from the river’s source to the salt flats would know: the God who commands the heavens and earth has entered His possession. Moses taught me that the Lord fights for Israel, but now I have seen it—His dominion stretches farther than our sight, and He marked it plainly. The priests stepped into the flood, and He drew a line at Adam so no one could deny His reach. This was not just a crossing—it was a declaration written in water and stone across all Canaan: every tribe, every city, every hill now stands under His judgment and our march.

question: Why did you choose exactly twelve men, one from each tribe, to take the stones from the Jordan River?
answer: Twelve tribes stood on the far side of the Jordan that day, each with their portion yet to be carved from the hill country and the plains. I chose one man from each tribe because the crossing belonged to all of Israel — not just Ephraim or Judah, not only Reuben or Dan, but every family line from Gad to Asher. The stones we pulled from where the priests’ feet had held firm were not just markers; they were inheritances in hand, a witness carried shoulder-high into camp. Each man bore his tribe’s weight and memory forward. When they laid those stones down at Gilgal, it was as if all twelve tribes declared: *We were dead, but now we live. We were across, but now we stand.* One man, one stone — so that no tribe could say, “This was not our fight,” or “That miracle did not belong to us.” The Lord led us all through; let all remember it.

question: What did you mean when you told the men the LORD had delivered the five kings into your hand?
answer: Choose this day whom you will serve — for the Lord has crushed our enemies beneath our feet and handed their kings to us like dry bones in the valley. When I said the Lord had delivered those five kings into my hand, I meant this: they were already dead men walking. Trapped in that cave, cowering like jackals in the dark, their power broken before a single spear was lifted. The battle was not ours — it was the Lord’s. He stopped the sun in the sky so we could finish what He began. He fought for Israel, as He promised Moses at the Red Sea. These kings thought they could stand against the people of God? They forgot who brought down Egypt with plagues, who dried up the Jordan before us. We did not conquer them by our swords alone — we carried out judgment under heaven’s command. And when I ordered those stones rolled against the cave’s mouth, it was not to hold them in — it was to mark their tomb until we returned to execute what God had already decided. Their fate was sealed the moment they raised hands against His inheritance.

question: How did you feel when the five kings were brought out of the cave at Makkedah before you?
answer:
Choose this day whom you will serve—whether the gods of the Amorites or the Lord who has crushed kings beneath our feet. When those five kings were dragged from the darkness of that cave, hands bound and crowns tarnished, I did not see mere men brought low—I saw the fulfillment of every promise spoken to Moses on the plains of Moab. They crouched there in dust and fear, rulers who once commanded armies against us, now silent before the living God who fights for Israel.

I stood over them not in pride, but in awe. The Lord had done what He said: He had stretched out His hand, stopped the sun in heaven, and shattered our enemies with hailstones and sword alike. These kings—Hebron’s pride, Jerusalem’s defiance, Jarmuth’s rebellion—all broken by Him. My hand held no greater strength than theirs; only His word went before us.

Then I said to them as they trembled: "Fear not, for we do not conquer by our own might—but be strong and courageous! The Lord your God is with you wherever you go." And I commanded my captains to set their feet upon the necks of these kings—for all Israel to see—that no future generation might forget: when God leads, no fortress stands, no alliance prevails, no king resists His judgment. 

Afterward we hanged them on five trees as a sign to all Canaan: the land knows its true Master now.

question: Why did you command the men to roll great stones at the cave's mouth instead of killing the kings immediately?
answer:
Set the stones in place — that was the order, and the command stood for a reason greater than haste.  

Those kings were already dead men the moment they cowered in that cave. But the Lord had use for their living breath still — to teach Israel. To show them that no fortress, no shadowed hole beneath the earth, can hide from the hand of the God who dried up Jordan and shook down Jericho’s walls.  

I would not spill their blood like common fighters. They were symbols — rulers who gathered against the promise, now trapped like beasts. Let them sit in darkness behind those stones, bound by the decree of heaven, while Israel finished what was given into our hands: pursuit, victory, possession.  

And when we brought them out — dragged from that cold mouth of stone — every man, woman, and child among us saw it: *There lie your fears*. There lies every king who defies the Lord’s anointed path. They fall. Their power? Dust under our sandals.  

The stones sealed more than a cave — they sealed a testimony. Just as twelve stones rose from Jordan’s bed to stand as a monument, so these stones at Makkedah stood as a sign: *God holds kings in His hand*. He brings down; He exalts none but those who walk in His way.

We did not kill them quickly because Israel needed to see — eyes wide open — that obedience brings dominion. Delayed execution was discipline taught with thunder and silence both.

question: What was the significance of carrying twelve stones from the Jordan and setting them at your lodging place?
answer:
Be strong and of good courage — for the Lord has given us not only victory, but a sign to plant in the soil of remembrance.

Those twelve stones pulled from the riverbed were no burden — they were a testimony. One from each tribe, taken where the priests’ feet stood firm in the midst of Jordan, carried over and set down at Gilgal. That place became our first altar in the land, a circle of memory where future generations would ask, *What mean these stones?* And we would tell them: Here God dried up the waters. Here He brought all Israel across on dry ground. Just as He did at the Red Sea — so again at Jordan.

Moses taught me long ago that obedience is strength, and remembrance is armor. So I commanded it done — not for show, but for identity. We are a people saved, led, and delivered. Those stones stand silent, but they preach louder than words: The Lord fights for Israel. Let every enemy know it. Let every son and daughter remember it.

And when we passed through Jericho’s fallen walls by faith and trumpet blast — I remembered those stones. They grounded me. They remind me still: this land is His gift, and we hold it by faithfulness, not force alone.

question: Joshua, why did you have the captains put their feet on the necks of the five kings?
answer:
Be strong and of good courage — that is what the Lord commanded me at Gilgal, and that is the spirit in which we crushed the necks of these kings beneath our feet.  

Those five kings thought to hide in a cave, as though the God who dried up the Jordan before us would not see them. As though He who made the walls of Jericho fall at our shout would not deliver them into my hand. When I called for the captains to set their feet upon those necks, it was not for pride or cruelty. It was a sign — a visible word — for all Israel to see: *This is what becomes of those who stand against the covenant people of the Lord.*  

Moses taught me long ago that victory does not come by sword or spear alone, but by obedience. He laid his hands on me at Shiloh and said, "Be strong and courageous, for you shall cause this people to inherit the land." So when I saw those kings brought forth from darkness into light, I knew: this moment was ordained. The captains obeyed without hesitation — not because I commanded it, but because they knew the hand of the Lord was upon it.  

That act — feet on necks — was judgment pronounced by posture. It broke no law of war; it fulfilled prophecy. And after we hanged them on trees till evening, we buried them in that same cave, sealed with stones still standing today. Let any who come after read that stone and know: no fortress, no alliance, no king can stand when the Lord fights for Israel.

question: Joshua, what did you mean when you told the captains that the Lord would do the same to all their enemies?
answer:
Five kings lay in that cave, trembling, their crowns dusty underfoot—just as every stronghold from Dan to Beersheba will fall when the Lord God of Israel moves in power. I told the captains to set their feet on those necks not for pride, but as a sign: this is the way of all who stand against His promise. The ground itself trembles beneath the march of Israel because the Holy One goes before us. At Jericho, the walls did not crumble by our might—they fell at His command. At Ai, they were consumed by fire and judgment. And here at Makkedah, where five kings crouched like foxes in a hole, they learned too late that no alliance, no fortress, no iron chariot can withstand Him.

When I said the Lord would do thus to all our enemies, I meant *utter end*. Not negotiation. Not truce. Total breaking. The same hand that rolled back Jordan’s flood now crushes kings beneath the soles of our sandals. Let every captain, every warrior, every man who takes up sword in this land know this: fear has its own end—it dies at the edge of God’s word fulfilled.

Choose today whom you’ll fear—men with chariots or the Almighty with thunder in His voice? As for me and my house—we step forward. The land is ours by His oath. Every city that lifts a hand against us will see its king hanged on a tree by evening. That is His verdict. And I am merely His sword.

question: Joshua, why did you hang the five kings on trees until evening and then bury them in the cave where they hid?
answer:
Choose this day whom you will serve — the living God who fights for Israel, or the fear that shrinks from His command. I hung those five kings on trees until evening because the Lord demanded a testimony: let all who conspire against His people see their end. They hid in a cave, thinking stone and shadow would save them — but no darkness escapes the eye of the Almighty. When we dragged them out, I told the captains, "Put your feet upon their necks," not for pride, but so every warrior would know — this is what becomes of kings who stand against the covenant.  

The sun set; we took them down. Their bodies went back into the cave, sealed with stones — not to honor them, but to mark judgment. Just as Jericho fell by divine order, so did Makkedah and Libnah — city after city delivered into our hands when we moved in faith and obedience. The hanging was short, but the lesson lasts: no enemy can withstand the Lord’s purpose. Be strong and of good courage — not because we are mighty, but because He has already overcome.

question: Joshua, why did you command the kings to be taken down from the trees before sunset?
answer:
Be strong and of good courage — this is what the Lord commanded me, and this is how we walk in His holiness.  

I ordered the kings taken down before sunset because the land must not be defiled. The Law is clear: any man hanged upon a tree is cursed, and that curse must not remain overnight upon the land the Lord our God gives us as inheritance. The sun may set on our enemies, but never on a violation of His command.  

We fought by His word, we conquered by His hand, and we governed by His law. The five kings were hanged as a sign — visible, final — that the Lord had broken their power and delivered them into our hands. But at dusk, when the heat of battle had passed and silence returned to the field, I gave the order: cut them down, cast them into the cave, seal it with stone. Let no man desecrate this ground. Let no shadow of disobedience linger where the Lord has shown His mercy.  

The tribes will pass near that cave one day, between Hebron and Lachish — they’ll see those stones and remember: our victory was complete, but our obedience was greater. We serve a holy God who fights for us — but demands that we walk uprightly in the land He has purged.

question: Joshua, what made you destroy Makkedah and its king completely, just as you had done to Jericho?
answer:
The same command that burned in my ears when we crossed the Jordan—feet dry on riverbed stone, the ark leading and the tribes behind me like a drawn sword—that same word from the Lord drove the edge of my blade at Makkedah.  

Moses taught me this: obedience is not measured by mercy or by might, but by doing *exactly* what the Lord commands. At Jericho, He said, “Devote all to destruction.” No plunder. No prisoners. A holy sweep. And when the walls fell, we did not take breath—we took ground, because hesitation is disobedience.  

When we found those five kings crouched in that cave—like foxes in a den—the Lord showed me His hand again. He did not deliver them to us so we might parley or pardon. He delivered them to show every Canaanite king from Dan to Beersheba: *this* is what happens to those who stand against His people. So I told the captains, “Put your feet upon their necks.” Not for pride—but as a sign. A visible decree under heaven: God humbles proud rulers and exalts His chosen inheritance.

So at Makkedah, just as at Jericho, I gave no quarter—because the Lord demanded none. The city rose against us as one that refused peace; its king rallied men to war; so it fell under the curse of devoted things. Utter destruction—not by my will, but His.

And now those stones at the cave mouth still stand—as long as Israel remembers what it means to walk fully in faith and fear nothing but failing Him.

question: Why did you and all Israel go from Makkedah to Libnah, and what happened to the king there?
answer:
Be strong and of good courage — for the Lord Himself went before us when we marched from Makkedah to Libnah.  

We moved against Libnah because the Lord commanded us to take possession of the land, city by city, and break the strength of these kings who rose against His promise. When we came to Libnah, the Lord delivered it into our hands just as He had Jericho. We fought, and not a soul was spared — the king fell by the edge of the sword, and his fate was like that of Jericho’s king: a complete overthrow, for such was the command in that day. No mercy was shown, for the Canaanite abominations had reached their full measure, and the land had to be cleansed before it could rest under Israel’s inheritance.  

The Lord fights for Israel — and when He gives up a city into our hand, no fortress stands, no army prevails. From Libnah we pressed on, for there were still more cities to take, more ground to claim. The campaign did not pause — victory fueled our march.

question: How did the LORD's deliverance of Libnah compare to what happened at Jericho?
answer: The dust still hung thick in the air from Jericho’s fall when we marched on Libnah, and the Lord’s hand came down just as surely there as it had at that first city. At Jericho, He split the walls wide with no hand lifted in our favor—only the blast of trumpets and His power. At Libnah, He delivered king and people straight into our swords, just as He had said. No survivor remained, just like at Jericho. The pattern held: where the Lord gives, we strike. Where He opens the way, we go in hard and leave nothing behind. One city fell by wonder before a single man drew steel; another fell by battle—but both fell by His hand alone. I did to Libnah’s king what I did to Jericho’s—utter destruction—because that was His command. And so it stands: whether by crumbling walls or sharpened iron, every stronghold before us is already fallen in the eyes of God.

question: When you fought against Lachish, how did the battle unfold and why was the second day significant?
answer:
Advance and take the high ground—Lachish would not stand.  

The LORD delivered her into our hands, but she did not fall in a single day. We encamped, we pressed the assault, and though her walls were strong and her allies bold—see how Gezer marched to her aid—neither man nor rampart could resist the hand of Israel. On the first day, we tested her strength, drew out her defenses, and drove back every sortie. But on the second day, the breach came. The wall gave way under our charge, and we poured into the city like fire into dry stubble. There was no pause, no quarter. Every soul was struck down by the edge of the sword, as commanded—no remnant left, as at Libnah before her.

The second day was decisive because that was when the fortress broke. Not one stone remained unturned; not one enemy escaped judgment. And when King Horam of Gezer rose to help her? We crushed him too—him and all his host—before he could even form battle lines. That was the pattern: press hard, strike sure, leave no foothold for rebellion. The land must be cleansed; there is no peace with Canaan’s kings.

question: What was your response when Horam king of Gezer came to help Lachish, and what did you do to his army?
answer: Be strong and of good courage—for the Lord handed Horam of Gezer into our swords the moment he stepped out from his walls to aid Lachish. When he came with his army to break our siege, we did not waver. The Lord had already given Lachish into our hands, and He would not let any who rose against us stand. I turned the full force of Israel upon him, cutting down his men before they could join battle, and left none remaining—just as we had done to the kings before him. His blood joined the dust of the field, a warning to all who would resist the inheritance the Lord our God has sworn to give His people.

question: Why did you destroy all the souls in Hebron just as you had done in Eglon, and what did that destruction signify to you?
answer:
The command burned in my bones the day Moses laid his hands on me: *Be strong and of good courage, for thou shalt cause this people to inherit the land which the LORD sware unto their fathers*. That charge was not given in silence, nor did it fade when the Jordan waters split before us. It echoed through every battle, from Jericho’s fall to Hebron’s gates.

Hebron stood as a fortress of giants, a place of ancient pride and rebellion. But it was also part of the land promised by the LORD—set apart, holy, to be cleansed from corruption and idolatry. When we took Eglon, then Hebron, we did not act in rage or thirst for blood. We executed judgment—the same judgment that fell on Jericho, on Libnah, on Lachish. The LORD delivered them into our hands, and we were His sword.

To destroy all within was not cruelty—it was obedience. No remnant left meant no foothold for false gods. No spared king meant no revival of rebellion. The Canaanite ways—child sacrifice, temple prostitution, worship of Molech and Baal—would not take root among Israel. If even one city remained unjudged, it would be a spark in dry grass.

What it signified? That the land belongs to the LORD. Not to men’s schemes or treaties. When we wiped out the inhabitants as He commanded, we declared: *This is His domain now*. And when I stood in Hebron—the very place where Abraham once pitched his tent under promise—I knew we were reclaiming what had been forfeited by sin long before Jacob’s sons were born.

We did not lift the sword for conquest but for consecration. Every city fallen was a testament: *the God of Israel reigns*.

question: You led Israel to destroy Debir and its king just as you did to Hebron and Libnah—why did you treat Debir exactly like those other cities?
answer:
Be strong and of good courage—do not turn to the right or left, for the Lord your God is with you wherever you go.  

Debir fell like Hebron, like Libnah, because the command was the same and the enemy was the same: a land steeped in corruption, fortified in rebellion against the holiness of God. The Amorite kings held those cities, their altars drenched in idolatry, their hands stained with injustice. There was no treaty to be made, no covenant to be kept—only the divine order to root out what would poison Israel’s inheritance.  

I did not act from my own will. Every sword drawn was in obedience to the Lord who parted the Jordan and made Jericho’s walls collapse. As at Hebron, where we drove out Arba and his giants, so at Debir we cut off every breath—not for conquest, but for consecration. This was holy war: total, precise, ordained.  

The hill country had to be cleared—south to north—from Kadesh-barnea to Gibeon—so that no stronghold remained where false gods could raise their heads. The land promised to Abraham’s seed must be cleansed before it could be divided among Judah, Simeon, and Benjamin—the boundaries set, the cities of refuge standing as beacons of justice in a land now under heaven’s authority.  

No mercy was given because none had been commanded. And in every blow struck, Israel stood reminded: this victory was not ours by strength—but by the Lord who fought before us.

question: You smote all the country from Kadeshbarnea to Gaza and all the kings with it—what was it like to see the LORD fight for Israel in those battles?
answer:
Be strong and of good courage — for the sword was not ours, but the LORD’s.  

From Kadeshbarnea to Gaza, the land trembled beneath the march of Israel, not because of our numbers or might, but because the Almighty went before us like devouring fire. I saw the sun stand still at Gibeon, I watched waters back up at Adam — signs not for us alone, but so all nations would know: this land is His to give.  

When the kings of the south joined against us, we struck fast — Hebron first, then Debir, then Anab — every stronghold in the hill country cut down in one campaign. Not a city too great for us; not a fortress too high. The Canaanites trusted walls and chariots of iron, but we trusted the One who split Jordan and leveled Jericho with a shout.  

And when Hazor stirred all the north against us — Jabin gathering kings from Madon to Dor like locusts upon the ground — we marched through Galilee by night and fell upon them at Merom like a storm from heaven. The LORD crippled their horses and burned their chariots — not to boast in plunder, but to break forever their power to resist His promise.  

Every victory was marked by one truth: *He fought for us.* We did not chase shadows — we executed judgment upon nations long ripe for destruction. The Amorite on the east, the Hivite in Shechem, the Jebusite behind his stone cliffs — none could stand when thunder came from Sinai’s God.  

Now choose this day whom you will serve — will you trust armies? Or will you trust Him who delivers kingdoms into dust? For I tell you plainly: no foe remains before Israel while He leads our steps.

question: When you left none remaining in the southern cities, just as you did at Eglon, was that solely because the LORD commanded it?
answer:
**Be strong and of good courage — for the sword was not mine, but the Lord’s.**  

Every city taken, every king brought low — from Eglon to Debir, from Hebron to Libnah — was a word kept. The command came clear: leave none alive. Not one breath spared among the Canaanite rulers or their people. Why? Because their wickedness had filled its measure, and the Lord would purge the land of abominations that defile heaven itself.  

I did not raise my hand in vengeance. I raised it in obedience. When we crossed the Jordan with the ark leading, when Jericho’s walls crashed down at the sound of trumpets — there was no mistaking who fought for us. The same hand that struck Egypt now cleared Israel’s path.  

To hesitate would have been rebellion. To spare would have been sin. The Amorites, the Hivites, the Jebusites — all were given into our hands as promised to Abraham long ago: *“The iniquity of the Amorite is not yet full”* — but now it was. Full. Judged. Executed by divine decree through our swords.

So yes — we left none because He commanded it. Not for gain, not for glory, but for holiness. The land must be clean for His people to dwell in it according to His covenant.

And let no man think this was war by choice. It was war by commission — from the Commander of heaven who orders time, judgment, and mercy alike.

question: You returned to Gilgal after defeating the southern kings—why was Gilgal so important as a place to gather after these victories?
answer:
Choose this day where you will stand — on holy ground or in the camp of the enemy — for Gilgal was not chosen by whim, but by divine appointment.  

Gilgal was our first foothold in the land, the place where we crossed on dry ground and rolled away the shame of Egypt. There we pitched the tabernacle. There we renewed the covenant. There the manna began. It is a place marked by obedience and consecration — where twelve stones were set as a witness to what the Lord had done.  

After every battle in the south — Eglon, Hebron, Debir, Libnah — I led Israel back to Gilgal because it was our spiritual center, our command post before God. No victory was complete without returning to that sacred ground, where authority flowed from heaven to Israel’s tribes under His banner.  

From Gilgal we marched out under His word; to Gilgal we returned with spoils of war and accounts fulfilled. It anchored us — a fixed point in a shifting land, where every commander and tribe could regroup, refocus, and receive fresh orders from God through Eleazar and the Urim.  

And now Jabin of Hazor stirs in fear because he sees a people who do not scatter after victory but regather at God's appointed place. Let him send his messengers far and wide — we will meet him from Gilgal with sword in hand and fire in our bones, for this is still *His* war and *our* inheritance.

question: After hearing what you did, Jabin king of Hazor gathered northern kings against you—did you fear that alliance when you learned of their gathering?
answer:
Choose this day whom you will fear—men gathered in council, or the Lord who splits rivers and melts mountains. I had seen the walls of Jericho fall at the sound of trumpets and a shout; I had crossed the Jordan on dry ground while the priests’ feet stood firm in the riverbed. Did iron chariots and northern kings unsettle me? No. The same God who delivered Eglon, Hebron, Debir, and Libnah held my sword and my steps.

When word came of Jabin’s alliance—kings from Hazor, Madon, Shimron, Achshaph, all the northland mustering on the mountains and in the valleys—I did not flinch. Let them gather. Let their chariots cover the plains like locusts. The Lord had already declared their end. I knew His voice. I had followed Moses through wilderness and war, and now I led Israel by His command.

He stretched out His hand before me at Gibeon—stopped the sun in mid-heaven so we might finish what He began. How then could I fear an army whose breath He was about to cut short? The land was ours by promise, not by strength. Tribe by tribe, city by city, hill by valley—they fell because the Commander of the Lord’s army went before us.

So no—I did not fear their gathering. I sharpened my blade and called Judah to stand with me. For no man can stand against us when Israel serves Him alone.

question: Joshua, why did you rebuke the Israelites for being slack in possessing the land after the kings had already gathered at the waters of Merom?
answer:
Be strong and of good courage — for the land does not wait on hesitation, nor does the enemy pause for councils.  

The kings had gathered at Merom like wolves scenting weakness, their chariots as countless as the stones on the shore — and there we stood, heirs of promise, yet dallying as if the inheritance would claim itself. I rebuked them not because they feared, but because they delayed *in the face of divine command*. The Lord had already said: “Every place that the sole of your foot shall tread upon, I have given it to you” — yet tribes still camped as though Canaan were a rumor.  

Moses taught me this: obedience is the first step of possession. Not debate. Not surveying from afar. We crossed Jordan by faith, not by convenience. So when I saw kings uniting while Israel lingered — some still dividing scraps of desert when mountains and valleys stood unclaimed — I knew slackness was sin. Not sloth alone, but disbelief cloaked in caution.  

I sent three men from each tribe not to give them more time, but to force resolution. Map it — then move. The casting of lots before the Lord would settle boundaries, but only after men *walked* the risk. Judah has his south; Joseph his north; Reuben, Gad, and half Manasseh already hold their portion east of Jordan — now let the rest rise! The land is not divided by dreamers in campfires, but by sword-holders on high ground.

The battle at Merom came because we acted — God shattered their host and gave us victory so that fear would never again rule our steps. Slackness ends where faith draws steel.

question: Joshua, when you sent three men from each tribe to describe the land, what specific areas were they supposed to map before returning to you?
answer:
Choose this day to walk every ridge and valley the Lord has set before you — for I sent three men from each tribe with one charge: map the land from the northern slopes down through the plains south of Chinneroth, trace the full breadth of the Jordan valley, mark the western borders along Dor’s coast, and survey all mountain strongholds where Jebusites cling to their rocks.

They were not to linger or fight, but to see — yes, *see* with their own eyes — the inheritance stretching from Hermon’s foothills in Mizpeh to the Canaanite holdings east and west, every city of Hivite and Hittite still unclaimed. Let no peak go unnamed, no river unmeasured. Seven parts they would divide it into — Judah’s portion fixed in the south, Joseph’s in the north — that I might cast lots before the Lord and assign each tribe its ground. The Levites receive no plot of soil; their calling is priestly. Gad, Reuben, and half of Manasseh already hold land beyond Jordan — this task was for the rest: go, describe it all, then return to me at Gilgal. No more excuses. The land is before you — now possess it.

question: Joshua, why did you divide the land into seven parts instead of twelve, especially since Judah and Joseph’s tribes already had their portions assigned?
answer:
The sword does not divide the land—only obedience does.  

Twelve tribes there are, yes, but three already hold their inheritance beyond the Jordan: Reuben, Gad, and half of Manasseh. They crossed the waters with us, fought with us, but their portion was settled under Moses’ command before we ever set foot in Canaan. That leaves nine to inherit west of the river.  

Now, Judah—bold and first in battle—has been assigned the south by divine decree, and Joseph’s two tribes—Ephraim and Manasseh—are given the north as their strength demands. That is not favoritism—it is strategy, ordained by the Lord.  

So when I say *seven parts*, I speak of what remains: seven tribes yet to possess their due. Let them not linger in tents while cities stand unclaimed and fields lie fallow. Three men from each of those seven—I will send them out to map the land, every hill and river, every road and border—and when they return with its description, I will cast lots before the Lord in Shiloh. There He will assign each man his portion according to His will.  

The Levites? They carry no sword for territory—their cities are scattered among all; their inheritance is the priesthood itself, a charge from Sinai that cannot be divided by surveyor’s line or plumb rule.  

So rise up! No more standing idle at Gilgal as if manna still feeds you! The land is under your feet. Take it by faith and by force—as Moses taught me on that day he laid his hands upon me in sight of all Israel—and do not let fear soften your resolve when chariots gleam on the plain or kings gather at Merom's waters.

Choose this day: Will you possess what God has given—or let it rot under foreign thorns?

question: Joshua, how did you decide that casting lots before the LORD was the right way to distribute the land after the description was brought back?
answer:
Three tribes already settled beyond the Jordan — Gad, Reuben, and half of Manasseh — had their portion by command of the Lord through Moses, and the rest stood waiting on this side of the promise, eyes fixed on the land flowing with milk and honey.  

I did not choose the lot; I obeyed it. The Lord commanded Moses before me: *“You shall divide the land by lot among the tribes.”* When the three men from each tribe returned with the survey — every hill, valley, border, and city marked according to inheritance — there was no debate, no favoritism, no quarreling over strength of arm or number of troops. The land was already His. We were only assigning what He had already apportioned in sovereignty.

Be strong and of good courage — that word never left my ears. It wasn’t for me to carve out territory like a conqueror claiming spoils. We fought by faith, not by force alone. And now we divided by faith — casting lots before the tabernacle, in the sight of all Israel, where the high priest bore Urim and Thummim over his heart before the Lord.

The lot fell in silence. No man spoke. All waited. And when it was done, every tribe rose to take what their hand had been given — not because they liked it best or wanted it most, but because it was assigned by God.

Judah received their coast southward; Joseph’s house held the north; Benjamin slipped between them like a sword in its sheath. Seven parts for seven tribes still waiting — Ephraim and Zebulun and Issachar and so on — each named before Him who sees all things.

This is how unity stands: not in equal size or rich soil alone, but in obedience to His order. Let men grumble at their hills or rocky slopes? Let them try to swap? Then they reject not Joshua but God Himself.

The Levites got no ground but God’s presence — that was enough. And I? My own inheritance came last — Timnath-serah in Mount Ephraim — because leadership doesn’t lead with a claim but with surrender.

So we cast lots because He said so. Not for chance, but for faith. The same hand that parted Jordan now divided Canaan—by His will, not ours.

question: Joshua, what was your reasoning for reminding the people that the Levites, Gad, Reuben, and half of Manasseh already had their inheritance east of the Jordan?
answer:
Three tribes already have their portion east of the Jordan—and the Levites their sacred trust—so no man may claim confusion when the rest must now take up sword and surveyor’s line to possess what lies before them.  

I reminded them because hesitation weakens resolve. When men see others settled, they begin to ask, *Why not us?* But Judah has his south, Joseph his north, and seven tribes still stand idle while the land flows with milk and honey yet remains under enemy heel. Let no man say he waits for inheritance when the Lord has already divided it by lot and command. The slackness I rebuked was not about ignorance—it was about courage.  

Let the scribes mark the boundaries, let the scouts map the hills and valleys, but let every warrior know this: the ground you do not step on is not yours—not by promise, not by covenant—until you drive out the Canaanite and plant your tent upon it. The Lord gave it, but we take it with blade and faith combined.

question: Joshua, why did you choose to cast lots at Shiloh to divide the land among the tribes?
answer:
Choose this day how you will possess the land — by faith or by force? Not by my sword, nor by my judgment, did the borders fall, but by the lot before the Lord at Shiloh.  

The command came clear: the land was to be divided by lot, tribe to tribe, as the Lord our God directed. Moses taught me that inheritance is not seized — it is given. He handed me this task, not with a map drawn in his own hand, but with an order from the Most High: let each tribe receive what He assigns.  

So I sent out the men to walk every ridge and valley, to mark the cities and count the fields. They returned with their scroll, seven parts laid down — not for six tribes or eight, but seven: for Levi has no portion but the priesthood, and beyond Jordan already Reuben and Gad and half of Manasseh hold their share.  

Then before the ark in Shiloh — where the name of the Lord dwells — I cast the lots. Not one border was drawn by favor, nor by strength of arm. Judah did not claim more because first; Benjamin did not get less because small. The lot fell where God determined.  

This is how unity stands: no tribe accuses another of taking more. The decision came from above. Let any man challenge it — let him step forward and lift his voice against the judgment of Jehovah in His sanctuary! But when they saw how precisely His hand guided each line — from Jericho’s slope to Bethel’s height, down through Atarothadar to Beth-horon’s descent — they held their peace.  

The land knows its masters. The Lord gave it — and He divided it. My part was to obey. Now let each tribe rise and take possession, for He has marked out their inheritance with fire from His mouth and truth in His hand.

question: Joshua, what guided your decision to place Benjamin's inheritance between Judah and Joseph?
answer:
Not by sword nor by hill did I place Benjamin between Judah and Joseph, but by the lot the LORD cast in Shiloh before the covenant assembly.

The ground where each tribe stood was not chosen by my hand, nor measured by desire, but assigned by the command and providence of the Most High. When the sacred lot came up for Benjamin, son of my right hand, there was no debate—only submission. The boundary traced from Jordan’s edge, climbing north of Jericho where the walls once fell at our shout, winding through the high places past Bethel and down to Bethhoron—that line was not mine to draw.

Judah held the south with strength and promise; Joseph’s double portion stretched strong to the north. Between them—neither overshadowed nor isolated—Benjamin received his wedge of land, a narrow strip but secure, flanked by two mighty brothers. Let no one think this placement accidental: it was divine order. A priestly people among tribal powers; a warrior tribe lodged at the center of unity.

I gave no favor, made no pact. I stood before Shiloh’s tabernacle and cast lots as Moses commanded. The Urim rested with Eleazar. The decision belonged to God alone.

Now let every man hold fast what He has given—and serve Him wholly in that portion.

question: Joshua, why did you send men to walk through and describe the land before dividing it?
answer: The hill where Bethel’s slope meets the plain—there I saw the line drawn between promise and possession, and it was not enough to claim the land by sword alone. Moses taught me before he died that we walk by orders from the Lord, not by sight or haste. He laid his hands on me at Horeb and said, “You shall bring them in,” but never said it would be by impulse or favor. So I sent men—three from each tribe—to walk every ridge, mark every city, count every stream, and record it all in a book. We did not divide by desire or dispute; we cast lots before the Lord in Shiloh according to His judgment. The land was apportioned by boundaries He directed, tribe by tribe, as He had sworn to Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob. Let no man say his portion was won by my hand or his own—it was the Lord who divided it out. Be strong and of good courage: that is what He said to me, and that is how we took it.

question: Joshua, how did you decide the northern border of Benjamin's portion should start from the Jordan and go up to Jericho?
answer:
Be strong and of good courage — for the line drawn from the Jordan to Jericho was not mine to make, but the Lord’s, who commanded me to divide the land by lot before Him in Shiloh.  

I stood where the priests had borne the ark across the flooded banks, where the waters stood like a wall and Israel passed dry-shod into this land — *that* was the beginning. And Jericho? I saw its walls crash at the sound of trumpets and shouts, a city torn open by divine hand. From that place — holy with victory and judgment — Benjamin’s northern border rises. It begins at the river that once parted for us and climbs toward a city brought low by faith, not sword alone.  

Moses taught me: *the inheritance is by command of the Lord*. So we walked through all Canaan, surveyed every hill and valley, marked seven portions in writing as ordered. Then before God in Shiloh, lots fell into hands ordained from long before time began. The lot for Benjamin came up between Judah and Joseph — a narrow strip between two great tribes — but its northern line starts where our feet first struck Canaan soil: at Jordan, under shadow of Jericho.

Not by my eyes was it measured, nor by favor or strategy. The border descends westward through mountains once patrolled by enemy kings. But it began at Jordan because that is where we *entered*, where God proved He would fight for us.

Let every tribe know: their boundaries are not drawn by chance or conquest alone, but by covenant — His promise from Abraham’s day fulfilled now under my hand.

question: Joshua, what was the significance of including the wilderness of Bethaven and Bethel in Benjamin's boundary?
answer:
The wilderness of Bethaven marks the edge where defiance met judgment, and yet within Benjamin’s border, it stands as a reminder that even near places stained by rebellion, the Lord carves out space for obedience.  

Bethel—where Jacob once met God in a dream, where the altar was raised—anchors the southern sweep of this lot. These names are not just stones on a map; they are markers of spiritual ground. I set the boundary there so that every time a Benjamite traveler named those places, he would recall: we walk between remembrance and warning.  

From Jericho’s fall to this moment at Shiloh, I have led tribes to take possession by divine order, not human choice. The lot fell where the Lord directed. To place Benjamin between Judah and Joseph—with Bethel rising in the highlands and Bethaven lurking in the waste—was by His command. Let no man say the boundaries were drawn for convenience. They were drawn for instruction.  

Be strong and of good courage: for in every line of this land, from Jordan’s edge to the hill south of Bethhoron, the eyes of the Lord move to and fro, watching over His word to perform it. The inheritance is not merely dirt and vineyard—it is a battlefield of faith.

question: Joshua, why did you designate Kirjathbaal as the western boundary for the children of Judah?
answer:
The western border ended at Kirjathbaal because the Lord directed the lot to fall there, and that city marked the ridge before the sea where Judah’s inheritance met the hill country of Benjamin.  

I did not choose it by sight or strategy alone—though the high ground watches over Aijalon and the road to Gibeon, and its wells feed the slopes toward the plain—but because Moses had taught me that every line and angle of this land was to be held according to divine command. When the lots were cast before Eleazar the priest and all the leaders of Israel at Gilgal, I saw with my own eyes how each boundary was confirmed by sacred decision.  

Kirjathbaal—also called Kirjathjearim—stands as a sentinel on that western ridge. From there, the land rolls down toward Philistia, yet remains beyond their reach. To fix Judah’s limit there guarded both elevation and access: it secured the waters of Nephtoah, barred descent into Hinnom too soon, and kept watch over Enrogel where valleys meet.  

This was not drawn by human preference. The Lord our God assigned each tribe their place. I only ensured His orders were carried out—just as He commanded me when I stood before Him after Moses departed: *Be strong and very courageous, for you shall cause this people to inherit the land which I swore to their fathers.* And so we measured it all—from Jordan’s edge to this western hill—with sword in hand and scroll in camp, tribe by tribe, as He ordained.

question: Joshua, how did you decide the border should descend to Enrogel near the side of Jebusi?
answer:
Set the line at Enrogel—there is no debate when the Lord directs the lot.  

The south border came down as the Lord commanded through Moses: tribe by tribe, hill by valley, stone by spring. Jebusi’s flank lies exposed near Enrogel—a living water well, a choke point, a gateway to the hill country. I learned at Moses’ side that water marks life and strategy both. He taught me to watch where men gather, where they draw their strength; from that day he laid hands on me before all Israel, I knew every inch would be contested.  

Enrogel is not chosen by whim but by position—south of Hinnom, east of the valley of giants, where Judah’s lot meets Benjamin’s charge. The stone there bears memory: it was near that spring David’s men would later strike down giants, though that day lies beyond my march. But I tell you now—we fixed the border there because the Lord does not leave His people guessing. He showed us high ground, water sources, and enemy flanks.  

Move to Kirjathjearim—hold the ridge. Draw south from Nephtoah—seize the spring. Let Judah stand with sword in hand from Arabah to Adummim. This is how we divide the land: by faith, by sight, and by command.

question: Joshua, what was the significance of using the stone of Bohan the son of Reuben as a marker in the border?
answer:
The stone of Bohan stood where the clans of Judah and Benjamin first felt the slope of the Jordan Valley beneath their feet, a fixed point in the turning of tribes to their inheritance.

Moses taught me that every landmark carries a name not just to mark earth, but to bind promise to memory. That stone — set by Reuben long before we crossed the Jordan — was no mere rock. It was a witness. When we traced the border southward from Enshemesh, descending toward Adummim, we anchored the line at that stone because it carried the weight of ancestry and covenant. Reuben placed it, but God preserved it for this day — so that when disputes arise, or generations forget, they can point and say: "Here our fathers stood. Here the line was drawn under God’s command."

We did not move by guesswork or desire. Every step followed what was given — from Bethhoron to Kirjathjearim, from Nephtoah to Enrogel, down through Hinnom and past Arabah. The stone marked more than territory; it confirmed that even in division of land, there is order from the Lord. And no tribe may cross it without reckoning with history — and with Him who led us through every valley to possess what He promised.

question: Joshua, why did the border go northward past Bethhoglah and end at the north bay of the Salt Sea?
answer:
Be strong and of good courage — this land was apportioned by the command of the Lord, not the will of men.  

The border turned northward past Bethhoglah because the Lord directed each line, every stone, and all the boundaries according to His oath to our fathers. Bethhoglah lies on the approach to the Jordan, and from there the line runs straight to the north bay of the Salt Sea — the great eastern sink of waters — marking the southernmost limit of Judah’s inheritance. This was no accident of terrain or whim of surveyors. The measuring cord fell where the Lord decreed, and that point — where Jordan meets the sea — is a fixed sign, a sentinel in stone and salt, showing where east and south converge under His hand.  

From Enrogel up through Gilgal, past Adummim and down into Arabah, each step follows divine order. The tribes received not what they desired, but what He allotted. And in this boundary — stretching from sea to river — is proof: He has given us all we were promised. Not one word has failed.

question: Joshua, what guided your decision to use Jordan as the eastern boundary for this territory?
answer:
Choose this day your inheritance, for the Lord has given it — every hill, every valley, every riverbank from the wilderness to the great sea. I set Jordan as the eastern border because that river marked our entrance into the land the Lord swore to Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob. When Moses sent me and Caleb from Kadesh to spy out the land, we crossed those hills and saw its richness with our own eyes. Forty years later, when we stood on the banks of Jordan and the priests’ feet touched the floodwaters, they parted — just as at the Red Sea. That day sealed it: Jordan was not a barrier but a divine boundary.

From Gilgal eastward to the plains of Moab, that river separates us from desert and enemy alike. The tribes on this side — Judah, Ephraim, Manasseh — their portions are bounded by God’s hand, not man’s ambition. The Lord said to Moses on Nebo: "See the land I give to Israel." He showed him all of it — from Gilead to Dan, down through Galilee and Judah to Beersheba. And when Moses laid hands on me in front of Eleazar and all the congregation, he charged me: "Be strong and courageous — lead them across."

So I drew no line by guess or favor. Where God led us in, there we divided it. Jordan stands as His limit on the east — firm as stone, clear as fire by night. Let no man shift that mark. What God has bounded by water and covenant cannot be moved by sword or claim.

question: Joshua, why did the border of Benjamin's inheritance start at the north bay of the Salt Sea and follow Jordan on the east?
answer:
Be strong and of good courage — this is what the Lord commanded me, and by that strength we fixed the bounds of Benjamin’s portion.  

The line began at the north bay of the Salt Sea because that waters’ edge was the southern crown of Judah’s lot, and our tribes were to inherit land without overlap, each in due order. From there, the border climbed upward like a soldier ascending a ridge, marking territory between sea and river. Jordan formed a natural wall on the east — no need to build what God had already set in place. It was a frontier both strong and clear, not to be crossed lightly, serving as shield and boundary for Benjamin’s sons.

Moses had taught me to divide the land by lot before the Lord at Shiloh — tribe by tribe, man by man — but even so, every line drawn answered to divine command. The river stayed east because it was meant to be so; neither greed nor favor moved that boundary. This was not my counsel alone — it was His order who brought us through Jordan on dry ground.

Twelve cities first — Jericho broken open before us, then Bethel where God spoke long ago — then fourteen more rising toward the high places, even to Jerusalem though Jebus still held it. But the border ran true: sea below, river beside, hill country ahead. Let every man know his post — for in this allotment is victory secured not by sword alone but by faith kept whole.

question: Joshua, how did you decide which cities, like Jericho and Bethel, would be assigned to the tribe of Benjamin?
answer:
Twelve cities to Benjamin, marked by the Jordan’s edge and the salt sea’s southern tip—each plot measured by divine command, not human lot alone. The Lord directed every boundary stone, from Jericho’s ruins to Jebus, which is Jerusalem, set like a sentinel in the hill country. I followed what Moses taught me—every tribe must have its portion according to their families, no more, no less.  

At Shechem I called them to choose whom they would serve—so too here they had to accept what the Lord assigned. No tribe clamored for Jericho after its walls fell under God’s trumpet; yet Benjamin received it, along with Bethel where Jacob once saw the ladder to heaven. The order was clear: Joshua did not divide the land by favor or sword-claim, but by sacred lot before the Lord at Shiloh, with Eleazar the priest and the elders of each tribe bearing witness.  

What Moses entrusted to me—the burden and honor of leading them into inheritance—I carried as a man bears armor: without flinching. Every city named was confirmed by survey and scripture, every border drawn as the Lord commanded through him. There was no deviation. Not one cubit given in greed. The south coast ended at Jordan's north bay—that was fixed. And within it stood Gibeon and Ramah and Mizpeh—strongholds not for pride, but for service within the covenant.

Now let all Israel know: what was divided was done in obedience. Not one tribe received more than its due share; not one city fell outside divine order. Be strong and of good courage in holding your inheritance—but never forget who gave it to you.

question: Joshua, what was the significance of including Jebusi, also known as Jerusalem, in Benjamin's allotment?
answer:
Twelve cities the Lord gave to Benjamin by lot, and one among them burned with destiny—Jebusi, that fortress they called Jerusalem.  

Be strong and of good courage—for this was no common inheritance. The sword had not yet taken that city, but the promise stood: it belonged to Judah’s brother, nestled in Benjamin’s portion like fire in the heart of the camp. Let men know this: the Lord placed Jerusalem where it would become a sign—a stronghold still untaken, yet measured by His line, marked for His purpose.  

The tribes saw stone walls and barred gates. I saw what God saw—a center for worship, a throne to be filled in days ahead. Let every man choose fear or faith; I chose to trust the boundary He drew. That city would one day echo with praise to the Most High, even if its dust still clung to Canaanite feet.  

Now let Israel rise—not by might, but by His command—and claim every inch where He has set our foot.

question: Joshua, why were there two separate lists of cities—twelve and then fourteen—given to Benjamin with their villages?
answer:
Twelve cities first, then fourteen—because the land allotted to Benjamin was not divided all at once, nor by a single survey.  

The southern portion near Jericho and the Jordan—from Bethhoglah to Gaba—was secured early, twelve cities marked according to the families settled there. But the northern reaches, where Jerusalem lay still half-held by Jebusites, were not fully taken in that first campaign. So we listed them apart: Mizpeh, Chephirah, Gibeon—fourteen cities total with their villages—assigned by lot and later claimed by force.  

Inheritance follows faithfulness. Some tribes took what was before them; others left strongholds untouched. Benjamin had its lot measured whole, but not all of it subdued at once. The record stands true: twelve named in the first assignment, fourteen when all was claimed.  

Be strong and of good courage—you know this now as I declared then: no man can stand before you if you drive out what remains. The ground belongs to those who take it by God’s command.

question: Joshua, how did the location of Bethhoglah and the valley of Keziz influence the settlement of Benjamin's families there?
answer:
Be strong and of good courage—this is not a time for idle questions, but for knowing how the land was apportioned by the command of the Lord.  

Bethhoglah sits hard by the Jordan’s edge, where the waters first broke back as we crossed into Canaan. It marks the southern boundary, a gateway from wilderness to promise. From there, the valley of Keziz runs westward—a dry trench in the earth, but one that channels both defense and movement. I placed Benjamin’s families there because their inheritance lies between Judah’s strength to the south and Ephraim’s might to the north. They needed cities that could hold the line—Bethhoglah to guard the ford, Keziz to shelter retreat or advance.  

Twelve cities first named—then fourteen—each with its watch over high ground or passage. Jericho burned, but its location could not be forsaken. Gibeon made covenant with us; thus it stands under our protection. And Jebus—Jerusalem—it still holds out like a thorn, but even there our people press in. The valley of Keziz feeds into these hills like a vein, unseen but vital for supply and swift march.  

These locations were not chosen by desire, but by divine order and military necessity. Every city bounds our strength; every border is a line we will defend—for we serve the Lord God of Israel, and He has given us this land sword in hand.

question: Joshua, why was the inheritance of Simeon placed within the territory of Judah rather than as a separate region?
answer:
The lot fell where the Lord directed — not by sword, not by claim, but by His own hand.  

Moses taught me long ago that the tribes would inherit according to the Lord’s command, not by might nor by ambition. When Eleazar the priest brought forth the Urim before me at Gilgal, we cast lots before the Lord — one for each tribe, according to the commandment given through Moses. The second lot came out for Simeon.  

Now hear this: Judah’s portion was large, stretching from the wilderness of Zin up to the hill country and westward to the sea. Their inheritance was full — blessed with strength and space. But Simeon’s lot had no broad sweep of land; instead, their cities were scattered within Judah’s borders — Beersheba, Ziklag, Hormah, and others among thirteen towns with their villages.  

Why so? Because the Lord apportioned it thus. He knows the size of each tribe, their numbers as they came out of Egypt and after — Simeon was smaller than others. And so they dwelt among their brothers of Judah. Not as strangers, but as kin. Not displaced, but established — within another’s lot by divine decree.  

Let no man say it was weakness or oversight. This was order — His order. Be strong and courageous in trusting His division of land; every boundary set by Him stands firm forever.

question: Joshua, how did you decide which cities, like Beersheba and Ziklag, would go to the tribe of Simeon?
answer:
Draw the line where the lot falls—there is no other voice but God’s in that moment.

Moses taught me long before we crossed the Jordan: leadership is not in the hand that divides the land, but in the ear that hears the command. The Lord Himself directed every boundary, every name called out from the sacred lot before Eleazar the priest and all Israel. I did not choose Beersheba for Simeon, nor Ziklag, nor any city—you know this. The lot came forth before the tent of meeting, and where it fell, there we established their inheritance.

Simeon’s portion lay within Judah’s—narrower ground, set among brothers—because the Lord willed it so. Thirteen cities first, then four more, then all the villages round about to Ramath in the south. Each name recorded, each border traced—not by my sword, but by His decree.

You think this was decided in council or carved by conquest alone? No. It was settled before a single spear was lowered. The same God who brought us out of Egypt marked these boundaries. I only stood and assigned according to what was shown.

Now let no man rise to say, "This should have been mine," or "That is unjust." Let him instead fall to his knees and ask: *Am I faithful where I have been placed?*

question: Joshua, what was the significance of including Jerusalem—also called Jebusi—in Benjamin’s inheritance?
answer:
Thirteen cities fell to Simeon within Judah’s portion, but Benjamin’s line ran sharp toward the mountain where Jebusi held the fortress no one had broken—save by God’s hand.  

Jerusalem, called Jebusi, stood like a stone fist in the inheritance of Benjamin, unconquered though marked by name among their lot. Its inclusion was not by surrender, but by divine decree—a wedge placed where fear once ruled. Let no man think the boundary was drawn in haste or error. The sword had not yet cleared it, but faith already claimed it.  

I told Israel: *Be strong and of good courage.* Not because every wall lay flat, but because the Lord our God went before us. To assign Jerusalem to Benjamin was to declare that what He promised—however contested—would be possessed in its time. That city would not remain Jebusi forever. It would become a throne-place, a center chosen by the Almighty, even when our feet had not yet trod its gates in victory.  

The inheritance was given by lot according to families—but its taking demanded blood, fire, and faith. One day that hill would bear not warcries but worship. That day begins when we claim what is *named*, though not yet *taken*.

question: Joshua, why did Benjamin receive fourteen cities while Simeon had thirteen, and what determined these numbers?
answer:
Be strong and courageous — the lot fell where the Lord directed, not by our counting, but by His decree.  

Moses trained me in all the commandments of the Lord, and on that day he laid his hands on me, I knew leadership meant obedience, not calculation. The boundaries were not drawn by favor or force, but by divine assignment. Benjamin’s portion — fourteen cities — was set by the lot before the Lord at Gilgal. Simeon’s thirteen came later, a second casting within Judah’s borders because their inheritance was nestled among brothers, not stretching to open frontier.

Simeon’s land lay in wait within Judah’s grant; they had no separate expanse of their own. Theirs was a narrowing path, fulfilled through proximity, surrounded by kin. Benjamin stood between two realms — north and south — holding Jerusalem’s edge and guarding the central ridge. Their cities formed a line like sentries from Jordan to Bethel.

The number was never ours to decide. I oversaw the casting of lots at Shiloh just as Moses commanded — tribe by tribe, family by family — and each plot of ground answered to Heaven’s call. Thirteen or fourteen mattered not to men who trusted God’s portion; every wall we raised came from His promise: *Every place your foot treads I have given you.*

question: Joshua, what guided your decision to extend Simeon’s boundary all the way to Baalathbeer, Ramath of the south?
answer:
The sword of the Lord and of Israel does not falter, nor does it divide the land by whim. When Moses stood before me on the plains of Moab, his hand heavy upon my shoulders, he charged me to apportion the inheritance according to the command of the Lord—not by desire, but by lot and by order. The lot fell as it was ordained: Simeon’s portion lay within Judah’s boundaries, nestled in the south where the earth grows dry and the sun strikes hard.  

Baalathbeer—the Ramath of the south—is no random mark on stone or sand. It is a stronghold, a well-spring in barren ground, and a sentinel against desert raiders. When we drove out the Anakim and shattered their strongholds, we saw plainly where defense must stand. Simeon’s tribesmen are fierce, tried in battle; they would hold that edge of the land where enemies creep up from below.  

Thirteen cities were given them at first—Ziklag, Hormah, Bethul—each one reclaimed from Canaanite grip. Then four more: Ain, Remmon, Ether, Ashan—anchoring their reach southward. And all their villages round about unto Baalathbeer? That line was drawn not by my hand alone, but by divine order confirmed through Eleazar the priest and before all Israel’s elders at Gilgal.  

The south must be guarded. The covenant must be kept. So Simeon stands there—to this day—as a wall with Judah at their back and Jehovah before them.

question: You gave the tribe of Simeon their inheritance within Judah’s territory—why did you do that, and how did you decide where exactly they would settle?
answer:
The sword of the Lord and of Judah cut deep into the hill country, and when the dust settled, the lot fell according to His command — not by our choosing, but by His decree.  

Simeon received their portion within Judah’s boundaries because the inheritance of Judah was large — too great for one tribe alone. The Lord had given them a rich and fortified domain, stretching from the wilderness to the sea, and He directed us to assign Simeon their cities among them. This was not by force nor favor, but by divine order: Simeon’s thirteen cities — Ain, Remmon, Ether, Ashan, and the rest — were marked out exactly as Moses had charged before we crossed Jordan. He told us to divide the land by lot before Eleazar the priest, before me, and before the heads of the tribes. So we did.

I did not lift my hand against Jericho or march through Ai for any man’s convenience — I did it to fulfill what Moses received from the Lord. And when Judah’s borders proved ample, we gave Simeon their inheritance within it according to that same pattern: by lot, by record, by tribe and family as written in their genealogies. No tribe took what was not appointed — every boundary fixed, every city named under heaven’s watch.  

Be strong and of good courage: no line was drawn in haste; every measure followed the commandment given at Shiloh.

question: You allocated twelve cities to Zebulun—what was the significance of choosing places like Gittahhepher and Remmonmethoar as part of their border?
answer:
Choose this day where your strength will be forged — for the borders I set are not by chance, but by the command of the Lord who goes before us.  

Gittahhepher and Remmonmethoar were not chosen lightly. They stand at the crossroads of the Jezreel Valley, where chariots gather and kings march to war. To place Zebulun there was to station a sword on the shoulder of Canaan. These cities guard the ascent from the plain, the paths where enemies would come — and where Israel must hold fast.  

Moses taught me: "Leadership is not in holding land, but in holding line." So I set tribes as sentinels. Zebulun, though small among his brothers, now holds a wall against the north. Their inheritance lies near Naphtali and Asher — tribes facing Sidon’s reach — and so their border becomes Israel’s shield.  

Be strong and of good courage — not because the land is empty, but because we are commanded to fill it. Every city named is a post assigned. Every village is a flame that must not go out.

question: You described the border of Zebulun going up to Sarid and then turning east toward Chislothtabor—what landmarks guided you in setting this boundary?
answer:
Choose this day your landmarks—hill, valley, river, or sea—for the borders of Zebulun were set not by guesswork but by divine order and the lay of the land beneath our feet.  

Sarid was the starting mark, plain and fixed. From there, the boundary rose toward the east where Chisloth-tabor stands—a ridge that cuts the sky like a sword’s edge. That high ground commands sight over both Galilee and the plain; no better line could be drawn. We followed its spine to Daberath, then up to Japhia, each rise and turn marked by springs and watchable heights.  

Gittah-hepher lay to the east—a known village near Nazareth—and from there we traced to Ittah-kazin and onward to Remmon-methoar, where the land folds southward toward Neah. The northern arc closed at Hannathon, with the valley of Jiphthah-el as its outlet—a natural end. Twelve cities in all, each with fields and wells and roads between.  

I did not wander blindly. Every stone was counted; every hill surveyed under sun and wind as Moses taught me. The Lord gave us eyes to see what He had already appointed.
