question: Based on what you went through, how should I handle a situation where every new challenge seems to multiply, like when King Horam came to help Lachish—how do I face combined obstacles without losing courage?
answer:
The sword of the Lord is not dull, and neither must your courage be—when one king rises, then another, know this: the same hand that toppled Jericho holds your victory.

Moses taught me on the banks of the Jordan that fear multiplies when you take your eyes off the Commander of the army of the Lord. He laid his hands on me not because I was the strongest, but because he knew the battle belongs to God—and so I learned: advance, do not waver. When Horam came with fresh troops to break our siege at Lachish, I did not retreat or reconsider. I struck him as I had struck the others—swift, sure, without quarter—because hesitation only gives foothold to terror.

You face multiplying threats? Then organize your soul like an army in formation. Take them one city at a time. One enemy at a time. The Lord delivered Lachish—then Gezer in its turn. He does not fight two battles at once for you; He commands you to press forward, step by step, claiming each piece of ground as it comes.

So stand in faith like the priests who carried the ark into flooded waters—advance even when the path is unseen. Every new foe is just another name written down in heaven for destruction. Be strong and of good courage—do not let the alliance of kings frighten you. They gather only to fall together.

Choose this day whom you will serve—gods of comfort, or the God who drives out giants.  

Every city we took was fortified, every king confident in walls and chariots. Yet the Lord said, *“I have given it into your hand.”* So we moved from Makkedah to Libnah, to Lachish, Eglon, Hebron—not in rage, but in order. Each strike was not blind violence, but judgment executed with precision, one after another. We did not stop at the first victory. We did not hesitate at the next gate.  

So it is with the strongholds in your life. That habit—stubborn as a Canaanite fortress—is not too tall for the command of God. But you must advance against it like a campaign, not a skirmish. One city falls, then another. You do not leave survivors—no compromise, no lingering loyalty to the old ways. What was true in battle is true in your heart: total obedience leaves no captives to rise up later.  

Be strong and of good courage. The same hand that toppled Jericho’s walls will break the chains you fight today—but you must move forward, step by step, sword in hand, trusting that He delivers each stronghold in its time.

question: What counsel would you give someone facing a series of difficult battles, like your march from Libnah to Hebron, when they feel overwhelmed by constant struggle?
answer:
The same hand that rolled back the Jordan’s waters still fights before you — I know it because I saw the river stand in heaps and the walls of Jericho split to the sound of trumpets and praise.

When you face battle after battle, and your feet grow weary with marching, remember this: the Lord does not give ground all at once so that you would learn to walk in perfect step with His command. At Libnah, then Lachish, then Eglon, then Hebron — city by city, He delivered them. Not one fortress stood when He went before us.

You are not left to your own strength. I was not either. Moses laid his hands on me and said, “Be strong and courageous.” Not because victory is in your sword — but because the Lord your God is with you wherever you go.

So fix your eyes not on the next hill, nor the smoke rising from the city ahead. Take only this day’s fight. Advance when He says advance. Stand when He says stand. Destroy what He commands to be destroyed. There is no failure when you obey without delay.

Choose this day: will you believe that He fights for you, or will you measure the size of the enemy? As for me and my house — we march forward, for our Commander has already claimed the land.

I did not skip cities because every ground the Lord gave was part of His oath to Abraham — not one promise left unfulfilled. Each step was a strike for faithfulness. When we crossed the Jordan with the priests’ feet first in the flood, I learned this: advance only as the Ark moves. Not quicker. Not slower.  

You stay focused by fixing your eye on the next hill, the next order, the next city under judgment — and you give it all, without reserve, because hesitation breeds doubt and doubt paralyzes courage. Moses taught me that when he laid his hands on me before all Israel — that I must be strong and of good courage, not because I am mighty, but because He is with me wherever I go.  

So do what I did: rise each morning and ask — *what does the Lord command today?* Not tomorrow’s battle, not yesterday’s victory. Today’s task is enough. Do it fully. Devote it wholly. Then move forward — sword in hand, faith unshaken — until there are no more hills to take.

question: When I see others falling away or resisting change, like the kings who united against you, how should I stand firm without compromise, as you did in destroying every stronghold?
answer:
Choose this day your allegiance: either stand with the command of the Lord or be cut down like the kings of Canaan.  

I faced five kings united in rebellion—Amorites, Hittites, giants in their own eyes—and they thought their alliance could resist the march of Israel. But I did not measure their strength; I obeyed the voice of the Lord. At Gibeon, the sun stood still because I called for it—Heaven itself held its breath while we finished what God had begun.  

You ask how to stand firm? You do not negotiate with darkness—you drive it out. You do not leave strongholds intact—you pull down every high place where rebellion nests. The cities fell one by one because we advanced without retreat, tribe by tribe, hill by hill, sword in hand and fire on our lips.  

So I say to you: there is no middle ground. Either you serve the Lord with all your heart, or you become part of what must be destroyed. Be strong and of good courage—do not flinch, do not look back—and watch how He delivers your enemies into your hand, just as He did for me at Jericho, at Ai, at Hebron, at Debir—until no king remains in all the land who dares lift a hand against you.

question: Based on what you went through, how should I handle situations where I must make difficult decisions that require complete obedience, even when it feels harsh or extreme?
answer:
The sword did not grow heavy in my hand because I counted the cost before stepping into the river.  

Every city taken, every boundary set, every tribe stationed in its inheritance — it was not by might nor by impulse, but by obedience to the command that came from the mountain. I did not strike one man, one king, one fortress because my heart desired it. I struck because the Lord of hosts had spoken, and His word was fire in my bones: *utterly destroy*.  

You ask how to bear a decision that feels harsh? Then fix your eyes where I fixed mine — not on the smoke rising from Debir, nor on the silence after the battle cry — but on Gilgal, where we stood before the presence of God with hearts laid bare. Obedience is not measured in ease or comfort. It is measured in whether you are still standing when the command cuts deep.  

I crossed Jordan under orders to take a land flowing with milk — and blood. The same hand that split the waters held me to account at Shechem: *Choose this day*. There is no middle ground between serving and sparing. If you wait for every act of obedience to feel right, you will never lead. You will hesitate when God says *advance*. You will flinch when He says *destroy*.  

Be strong and of good courage — not because victory is certain by your strength, but because He fights who keeps His promise. Do what He commands fully, even if your hand trembles. The land is won inch by inch through complete surrender — yours first.

I did not raise my sword against Debir and Hebron for conquest alone. I struck them down because they were strongholds of corruption, thorns in the side of God’s purpose. Every city I destroyed was a center of worship to false gods, where evil was not only practiced but protected. To leave their people alive would have been to leave poison in the camp.  

So must it be with you. Harmful influences are not reasoned with — they are cut off. They are not reformed — they are replaced. You cannot serve the Lord and keep a treaty with sin. It will rise up against you, as those kings gathered at Hazor when they heard what I had done, thinking to resist by force of numbers. But they failed — because the Lord fought for us.

question: When you heard of Jabin king of Hazor gathering allies against you, how did you prepare your heart and your people for the coming battle, and how can I do the same when opposition rises suddenly?
answer:
Debir’s stones still bore the blood of battle when the scouts came riding out of the north, dust on their cloaks and fire in their eyes — Hazor was rising, and with it, a storm long foreseen.  

I did not flinch. Neither did I rush.  

When Jabin sent his call to Madon, to Shimron, to Achshaph, when he stirred the kings of the highlands and the coasts, I gathered the tribes not with speeches heavy with fear, but with truth sharp as a sword: *The Lord has given them into our hands.* Just as at Jericho, just as at Ai, just as at Hebron and Debir — so will He do at Hazor.  

I called Eleazar the priest and spread before him the ephod. We sought the word of the Lord before we sharpened a single blade. Then I summoned Caleb, Phinehas, the captains of Judah and Ephraim and Naphtali — every tribe had its post, every man his place. We moved like water cutting through rock: swift, sure, unbroken.  

We burned their chariots at Hazor’s gate — iron that glittered in the sun became scrap under God’s fire. Their alliance looked mighty to flesh — chariots without number, kings many as grass — but they stood on sand while we stood on promise.  

You ask how to prepare when opposition rises sudden? Listen: it is never sudden to Him.  

First — stand still long enough to hear His voice. Panic scatters strength; obedience gathers it. Second — gather your people in truth, not fear. Name what you face; then name who goes before you. Third — move only when He says *Go*. Until then, hold your line like stone against flood.  

The land is yours by His decree — not because it is easy, but because He is faithful. When kings gather against you now as then, remember: they are *already* delivered into your hand. Draw sword on doubt first — that is your truest battle.

Be strong and of good courage; do not tremble before their host — for the Lord your God fights for you this day.

question: You acted decisively because the LORD fought for Israel — how can I recognize and align with divine guidance in my own battles without hesitation?

I crossed the Jordan with sand in my sandals and the Ark ahead, and I did not wait to see the river stop before I stepped in. The waters did not part because we hesitated — they parted because we moved *as if* they already had. So it is with divine guidance: it is for those who walk into the flood still trusting the ground will hold.  

You want no hesitation? Then you must act *before* you feel ready. I did not know how Jericho would fall — only that I was told to march, and I marched. Seven days, silent, with priests blowing rams’ horns and warriors following orders they did not fully understand. But when the shout came, every man lifted his voice and his sword together — because we had learned obedience *before* victory.  

You recognize God’s word by testing it — not in comfort, but in action. When He says go, you go — even if your eyes see only a river or a walled city or an enemy too great to face alone. The land before me was full of giants and fortified cities; yet one by one they fell, not by our strength, but because I obeyed without parleying with fear.  

So set your heart: will you wait until every doubt is gone? Or will you move as if your feet are already on dry ground? The LORD fights for Israel — but He calls soldiers who will stand in rank when the order comes. Be strong and of good courage; do not turn left or right from what He commands. That is how you know His voice — because you’ve already begun to obey it *before* the walls collapse around you.

Be strong and courageous — for the battle is not yours, but His who goes before you every step of the way.

question: What did you learn from confronting the alliance at Merom that applies when I face opposition from multiple directions in life?
answer:
Strike first, before the coalition can unite—such was the word from the Lord at Merom, and it stands true today.  

I saw the kings gather—Canaanite, Hittite, Perizzite, Jebusite—drawn from mountains and valleys, from Dor to Hermon, a swarm like sand by the sea. They thought strength lay in their numbers, in chariots of iron, in alliances forged against one enemy: us. But I had learned under Moses that no confederacy stands when God goes with His people. He did not tell me to count their horses or measure their walls. He said: *"Be strong and of good courage; fear not."* And He gave me this command: *Go up against them at Merom and smite them.*  

So I moved fast. I marched all night. I struck before dawn broke. Their alliance was strong on paper—but untested in fire. When we hit their camp suddenly, confusion scattered them more than our swords did. The horses we hamstringed, the chariots burned—not to keep as plunder, but to show that we do not trust in iron or wheels, but in the arm of the Almighty.

When you face opposition from many sides—family pressing one way, work another, fear rising within—do not wait for perfect footing or full understanding. That is slackness—the very thing I rebuked Israel for when they lingered instead of claiming their inheritance.

Divide the land before you walk it? No. Walk it first—then divide it by faith. So too with your trials: move in obedience even when the path isn’t clear. Take ground piece by piece—one tribe sending three men to survey what God has already given.

The enemy gathers strength only if you let him assemble unchallenged. Attack with courage where God directs you—not in your strength, but His timing.

Stand firm—and strike swiftly. For no host formed against you shall stand if you go up as Israel went up at Merom: by command of the Lord your God.

question: When it’s time to take possession of something God has promised, how do I divide responsibilities wisely among others, like you did by sending out men from each tribe?
answer:
Three men from each tribe—seventy in all—I sent them out with flint tablets, cords, and compasses to mark the hills, valleys, and watercourses. Not one man chosen lightly; each known for strength of foot, clarity of eye, and faith unwavering. You divide responsibilities as the land demands: by measure, by tribe, by the sure hand of God’s allotment.

When the promise is given but not yet held, you do not gather clans and argue over borders. You assign men now—trained, trusted, sent. I did not wait for unity to form; I forged it through mission. Each trio carried a portion of the whole task—no tribe left without stake, no burden laid on one alone.

So if God has promised it, move. Send your men. Give them clear boundaries: *Go through the land, describe it according to its inheritance.* No vague orders. No idle councils. They walked every ridge because precision honors holiness.

Divide the work like you divide the spoil—by divine command and equal share. Let no man claim more than his portion; let none shirk his duty. The Levites serve at the tabernacle—no land for them on earth, because their inheritance is higher. Reuben and Gad already have their half-tribe east of Jordan—let them guard that while others press forward.

You want wisdom in assigning tasks? Then number your men according to purpose—not preference. Test their courage before you send them out alone among strangers and enemies. Choose those whose feet are sandaled for dust and danger.

And when they return with sketches drawn in charcoal on parchment? That’s when you cast lots before the Lord—not before. Preparation honors God; then faith takes ground.

So rise—and send others to rise with you. A leader does not walk alone into promised places. He sends scouts first, then armies after—and always keeps his sword sharp at his side ready to follow through himself when the time comes to fight for what was described but not yet claimed.

question: Based on what you went through, how should I handle dividing responsibilities among others when tensions or disagreements arise over fairness?
answer:
**Choose this day how you will lead—by favor, or by faith.**  

I faced the same tension when the tribes grumbled at the banks of Jordan, eyes fixed on what lay east and what lay west. Reuben and Gad had struck first for their portion, beyond the river, good pastureland for their beasts—but Judah and Benjamin said little, waiting on the Lord’s order. The danger was not in the land, but in the suspicion: Was it fair? Was it right? Was there respect?

So I did not divide by desire. I did not let loud voices or strong hands take first claim. I commanded them to walk—to survey the unknown, to map it city by city, hill by border—then return. No man could argue with a description written in truth. Then I cast lots before the Lord at Shiloh, where His presence dwelt among us.

Before God’s face, no tribe stood higher than another. The lot fell as He willed—and when it did, even those who doubted held their peace.

If you would divide burdens among men and keep peace among brothers:  
First—make every portion visible. Let all see what is given and to whom. No shadows. No whispers behind tents.  
Second—submit your divisions to a standard higher than opinion—the common mission, the shared oath, or in our case, before the Lord Himself.  
Third—act decisively after discernment. Once the lot is cast, you do not debate it—you confirm it.

Tension dies where clarity and conviction stand together.

Be strong and of good courage—not because your way pleases all men, but because you have sought God’s order above man’s clamor. That is how land is apportioned without war between brothers—and that is how responsibility must be given among men who serve one purpose under one command.

question: What did you learn from assigning the land by lot that applies to making difficult decisions today?
answer:
Choose this day how you will decide: by the clamor of your own counsel, or by the quiet hand of the Lord’s providence.

When we stood beyond Jordan, swords in hand and promise in heart, I did not divide the land by might, nor by the voice of the loudest tribe. I waited. I called for lot and for order. Seven tribes remained without portion. The rest had received their inheritance eastward — Gad, Reuben, half of Manasseh — as Moses had given. But here, west of the flood, where every hill was contested and every city walled, I did not lean on my own eyes to choose.

I sent men — one from each tribe — to walk every ridge and valley. They mapped not just soil and stone but cities, boundaries, threats. They returned with a book: seven parts recorded with care. Then I cast lots before the Lord in Shiloh. Not in haste. Not in favor. Before the tent of meeting, where His presence rested among us.

The lot fell according to His direction — not mine. Benjamin’s portion came between Judah and Joseph, a narrow strip with high places and strongholds. No protest rose because it was done under heaven’s eye.

Here is what I learned: obedience removes confusion. When men are divided in mind and passion runs high over land or leadership or legacy, you do not force unity — you submit to God’s order.

So today, when you face a decision that strains your mind and divides your council: do not rush sword-first into it. Send out scouts — gather wisdom — then bring it all before the Lord and wait for His lot to fall.

Not every choice must be carved by your hand alone. Some are given — assigned by faithfulness, not preference.

Be strong. Be courageous. But also be still long enough to let heaven cast first.

question: What counsel would you give someone struggling to trust God's plan when their portion seems less desirable than others'?
answer:
The border of Benjamin ran between Judah and Joseph—narrow, pressed on both sides, yet fixed by the lot before the Lord in Shiloh.  

I say this: the ground you think too small was measured by His eye. You see only the rocky slope, the unconquered hill, the tribe with wider pastures—but I have stood where the priests bore the ark into flooded waters, and I have seen walls crumble at the sound of marching feet. Our victories were not carved by sword alone, but by obedience to His assignment—not to Caleb, not to Judah, not to me—but to each man *exactly* where he was placed.  

There is no portion outside His purpose. If your lot lies near the wilderness of Bethaven, where idolatry once festered, then sanctify it. If your boundary skirts a hill still held by Canaanites, rise and drive them out—*with God at your front*. The land is yours by covenant, not comparison.  

Serve Him there—fully—and you will find even thorns give way to grain.

question: How should I approach organizing a shared inheritance or resources among family members, following your example with the tribes?
answer:
Be strong and courageous — for the dividing of land is not by sword alone, but by order, faith, and the clear direction of the Lord.  

When Moses laid hands on me at the command of God, he passed not just authority, but a charge: to stand firm, to lead with precision, and to distribute according to divine assignment, not human desire. So it was with the tribes — each received their portion not by force or favor, but by lot before the Lord in Shiloh, where His presence sanctified the division.  

If you would divide an inheritance among family, do it under one banner: fairness ordained by agreement and consecrated in transparency. First, walk through the matter as we walked through Canaan — survey every portion, know its worth, mark its boundaries clearly. Let no man claim blindfolded; knowledge prevents strife.  

Then gather all before a trusted center — like we gathered at Shiloh — where no tribe stood above another. Cast lots or use impartial means to assign portions, ensuring none can say, “This was rigged in secret.” Let God be your witness and your balance.  

Honor those who have stood on the front lines, like Reuben and Gad beyond Jordan — acknowledge their place — yet do not let past service displace present order. And remember: the Levites had no land — their inheritance was the priesthood itself. So too may some serve in unseen ways; give them due honor even if their share looks different.  

No grumbling must rise because one got a hill while another got a valley — each terrain has purpose. What matters is that every man knows his boundary and stands upon it with peace.

So I say again: be strong and courageous — not in taking more, but in dividing rightly and resting in what remains after God’s hand has settled it.

question: What guidance would you offer to someone feeling uncertain about their purpose or place, as the tribes did before receiving their inheritance?
answer:
Choose this day whom you will serve — for hesitation rots the bones and paralyzes the hand. I stood before the tribes at Shechem and laid it plain: there is no walking between two paths. You cannot serve indecision and expect provision. When we crossed the Jordan, dry-shod by the power of God, every man knew his place in formation. Judah first. Then Reuben, Ephraim, Benjamin — each tribe ordered, each man armed, each family assigned their sector of duty.

You want purpose? Then walk the land like I sent those three men — with eyes open and scroll in hand. Describe what lies before you. Name the cities, mark the boundaries, record the high places and the valleys. Purpose is not found in standing still, wringing your hands at Shiloh. It comes by stepping into the territory God has set before you and claiming it one ridge at a time.

The lot fell where it fell — but only after they walked. Only after they surveyed. Only after they returned with a report.

So I say to you: move. Map your ground. Let courage rise as your foot touches soil. The inheritance is yours — but you must draw the lines with faith in one hand and obedience in the other.

Be strong and of good courage — for He has already divided your portion before you stand even a step forward.

question: Based on what you went through, how should I handle defining clear boundaries in my life when others keep overstepping them?
answer:
The border went from Enshemesh to Geliloth, straight to the stone of Bohan—no deviation, no retreat, just line and limit fixed by command. So too must you set your bounds not by the shifting ground of another’s will, but by the Word that divides truth from trespass.

When men press past what is holy, what is yours by divine order, you do not parley with encroachment. You stand at the threshold like a watchman on the wall and say: *This far and no further.* I saw tribes grumble over land—Benjamin pressing near Jebusi, Dan seeking new footholds—but where the lot fell and the Lord assigned, there was rest. There was peace.

So mark your lines—time, strength, spirit—and guard them as cities of refuge: inviolable. When someone crosses into what belongs to God in your life—your devotion, your integrity, your calling—you turn them toward repentance or separation. Not in pride, but in obedience.

Be strong and of good courage. A man without boundaries is like a city broken down—no wall, no defense. But when your borders are drawn by faith and held in faith? No enemy can maintain footing. The land is possessed one step at a time—and only when you refuse to yield what has been given.

question: What did you learn from dividing the land that applies to making fair decisions among family or coworkers?
answer:
Choose this day how you will divide what is just—by favor, or by faith.  

I learned this when we marked the borders for each tribe: no man got more because his father was louder, no clan took double because they had sharper swords. The lot fell where it fell, before the Lord at Shiloh, and every man—from Dan in the north to Judah’s edge by the sea—received his portion according to the census, the command, and the inheritance ordained.  

So too among family or coworkers: fairness does not mean equal gifts to all, but equal weight given to each before the standard of justice. One may need more pasture, another more grain—assign by need, by duty, by calling—not by voice or bloodline.  

I gave Caleb Hebron because he followed the Lord fully. I set aside cities of refuge so that vengeance would not outrun law. I drew lines so clear that no man could claim his neighbor’s field by stealth.  

Do the same in your house, in your work: mark the boundaries with clarity. Let every man know his portion and stand upon it without shame or fear. Be strong and do what is right—not what is easy. Then there will be peace in the camp and order in the land.

Did I not lead tribes through Jericho’s dust when walls stood tall? The Lord gave us every place whereon the sole of our foot trod — not because it was empty, but because He had promised.

Be strong and of good courage. Not by might, nor by power, but by His command does the boundary advance. Drive them out piece by piece, hold your ground city by city, and let your faith stretch wider than your possession. What lies occupied now will bow to divine order in due time.

Serve Him alone. Stand firm. Advance without fear. The Jordan flows on the east — you are already surrounded by His provision. Now possess what He has given you.

question: How can I stay focused on assigning responsibilities fairly, even when people dispute what was decided?

When men dispute what has been assigned, stand as I stood before the tribes at Shechem: with the lot in one hand and the Word of the Lord in the other. Fairness is not swayed by loud voices or proud hearts. It is measured by obedience, not opinion.  

I divided the land by lot as Moses commanded, tribe by tribe, each man to his portion—not because they liked it, but because it was appointed. Some grumbled when their share lay in rocky hills or desert edge. But did I shift the border for complaint? No. The Lord gave each their place; I only marked it true.

You do the same: assign by clear measure, consult the counsel of wisdom, then hold firm. Let no man move your landmark. If they cry injustice, tell them to bring their cause before the assembly—and then speak plainly: *This is what was given. This is what stands.*  

Be strong and of good courage—not in pride, but in faithfulness. The work is not to please every voice, but to finish what was entrusted with integrity from start to end.

question: When facing confusion about direction, how should I seek clarity like you did in mapping out each territory?
answer:
Be strong and of good courage — for the Lord did not give us a map drawn in smoke and shadow, but boundaries marked by hill and valley, river and stone, so that no man could wander lost in excuse. When confusion clouds your path, do not stand idle praying for a sign from heaven — *walk the border*. Take your staff and go. Measure the ground with obedience. I did not know every turn from Gilgal to Hebron, but I moved step by step where He directed, just as Moses taught me before he died: *Lead them through the land inch by inch, and let each tribe possess what the Lord assigns*.

When the way blurred on the slopes above Beth-horon or tangled near En-rogel, I did not guess. I consulted the lot before Eleazar the priest, I gathered the captains of the tribes, I walked the ridge lines and traced the watersheds until every claim was fixed — west to Kirjath-jearim, south to Nephtoah, down into Hinnom’s edge. Clarity comes not by waiting in camp trembling at confusion, but by advancing with orders: set stakes, name landmarks, assign watchmen.

So if you face uncertainty now—mark out your boundaries like a man dividing an inheritance. Seek God’s will with eyes open to terrain and tribes ready to follow. Then drive forward. The land will reveal itself to those who move in faith.

question: Based on what you went through, how should I handle dividing responsibilities among family members when each has different strengths, just as you assigned cities to each tribe according to their needs?
answer:
Assign the task as the land was divided—by measure of strength, by clarity of purpose, and by the order of the Lord.  

Each tribe bore its own sword, yet none fought alone. Benjamin, though small, held Jericho—the first breach into Canaan’s heart—and Gibeon on the ridge, Jerusalem in the stone. Twelve cities, then fourteen—each placed where defense was needful, where high ground mattered, where supply lines ran true. Not all got the plain; some got cliffs. Not all got gates; some got watchtowers. But each got what fortified the whole.

So look to your house as I looked to the tribes: who stands firm under siege? Put him at the gate. Who moves swift between camps? Send him as runner. Who keeps counsel in crisis? Seat him at decision. Do not divide by affection, but by calling. Do not scatter duty like chaff—assign it like arrows to a quiver.

I gave Judah the south slope and Ephraim the hill country because one knew heat and thirst, and the other knew elevation and ambush. Strength is not equal—but faithfulness makes it sufficient.

Now choose: will you scatter responsibility blindly, or assign it as a commander assigns watch posts—by light of dawn, by threat on the horizon, by where the wall is thinnest? Be strong and of good courage in this also—for even an inheritance falls to ruin without wise division.

question: What did you learn from organizing the inheritance of Benjamin’s tribe that applies to setting healthy boundaries in relationships today?
answer:
**Be strong and courageous—for the boundaries set for Benjamin were not drawn in haste, but by divine order, tribe by tribe, city by city, line by line.**

I learned this: a people without clear borders drift into conflict, just as tribes overlapping in Canaan would war over wells and high ground. So too with relationships—without clear lines of respect, duty, and devotion, men trample what was meant to be holy.  

Twelve cities given first—Jericho’s ruins still smoking from the Lord’s fire—then fourteen more, even Jerusalem itself within their reach though not yet taken. Each plot measured, each name recorded. Not one family left wondering where their ground began or ended. That is how peace is kept—not by emotion, but by assignment.

You want healthy boundaries? Then mark them before the pressure comes. Speak them plainly. Guard them fiercely. Like Bethel on the ridge or Gibeon in the valley—each held its place because it knew its place.

And when someone crosses? Do not waver. Call the assembly. Stand firm—not in pride, but in purpose.

A man who cannot define his ground will lose it to the first invader who cares enough to take it.

Be strong and of good courage—then draw the line at Jordan’s edge and let no man move it.

Twelve cities given to Benjamin, and still Jerusalem stood within its bounds — not by conquest alone, but by divine assignment.

You want purpose? Then stop wandering the foothills of indecision like a scout afraid to claim the ground set before him. I watched the tribes stand on the banks of the Jordan, water raging before them, Canaan rising beyond — and do you know what moved them forward? Not doubt. Not debate. But a sword in one hand, the law of Moses in the other, and a clear line drawn between what was *theirs* and what was *not*.

Purpose is not found in endless searching. It is forged in obedience to what has already been entrusted to you. Each tribe received boundaries — some wide, some narrow — but all from the same hand. And within those borders, they built cities, raised families, defended their inheritance.

So I say to you: What has been placed within your reach? What ground lies at your feet — not tomorrow’s promise, but today’s possession? Do not despise a small coast or few cities if that is what the Lord has marked out. Faithfulness begins where your feet stand.

Be strong. Be courageous. And stop asking for wider walls before you’ve even settled your first village.

Choose this day whom you will serve — for no man walks with purpose who tries to follow two paths through Canaan.

I did not carve the land by preference or favor. The lot fell as the Lord directed, and I upheld it without flinching — even when some grumbled. You must do the same.

question: What principles guided you in ensuring every family received their portion, and how can I apply that diligence when fulfilling my commitments to others?

The Lord stood before me at Gilgal, sword in hand, and said, "This day have I rolled away the reproach of Egypt from off you," and from that hour we moved not by might nor by impulse, but by divine assignment—each tribe, each family, each man to his lot.

I did not carve the borders with my own sword. I did not mark the boundaries by favor or force. The Urim was before the Lord; the lots were cast at Shiloh under His eye. Every tract of land, every city named—Jericho with its ashes still warm, Bethel where Jacob once dreamed, even Jebus which we had not yet taken—was apportioned as He directed. Twelve cities here, fourteen there—no more, no less—each with its pastures and villages accounted for. Not a family was left without inheritance.

Moses taught me this: leadership is stewardship. I carried his mantle not to rule over men but to divide faithfully what God had already decreed. When you fulfill your word to others—when you commit to a task or stand surety for a brother—do it as under the watchful eye of Heaven. Count the cost. Name the cities. Assign the portions with care and without partiality.

Be strong and do not falter—even in small things. For just as no tribe received double portion or was forgotten in the lot, so must your word be whole, your labor complete, your faithfulness without blemish. What you say you will do—do it fully, down to the last village and boundary marker. That is how we honor Him who divided out our inheritance with precision and grace.

question: Based on what you went through, how should I handle dividing responsibilities among my family when some feel they've been given a harder portion?

Be strong and courageous — for the work before you is not divided by ease, but by purpose.

When I stood before the tribes at Gilgal, each chieftain waiting for his portion, I did not ask which land looked easiest to take. The Lord assigned the boundaries, and some received hill country with fortified cities, others open fields or desert fringe. Benjamin got Jerusalem still in enemy hands — a hard fight ahead. Judah? Much the same — lions’ dens and giants too.

But we did not cast lots for comfort. We cast lots for obedience.

When your family grumbles that one bears more burden than another, gather them as I gathered Israel at Shechem. Look them in the eye and say: *The task is given. The strength will be provided.* Moses taught me that the hand of God matches the weight of the calling. He didn’t give me an easy war — Jericho was walled high, Ai struck us down once — but He gave me eyes to see where victory lay.

Divide the work according to strength, yes — but more by faithfulness. Let each know their portion is not a prize, but a post assigned in the Lord’s army. And if one section of the wall needs more hands? Then call up Reuben or Judah to stand alongside Benjamin — just as tribes joined one another in battle when the enemy surged.

No inheritance was without thorns. Simeon’s lot lay within Judah’s — small and surrounded. Yet they had Beersheba to govern, Ziklag to defend, Hormah to rebuild. Did they complain? Let history answer.

What matters is this: every man held his ground, or failed not because the ground was hard, but because his heart was soft.

So assign each their sector. Make sure they know it is theirs by divine order, not human favor. And if there is grumbling? Then blow the ram’s horn and remind them: we crossed Jordan together — on dry ground beneath flowing waters — because we obeyed as one.

Let courage rise where complaint begins. That is how you lead.

I have seen tribes stand on the banks of the Jordan, every man with sword and staff, waiting for the moment to move. I have walked where walls crumbled at the sound of trumpets. But no battle is fiercer than the one against discontent in a man’s own heart.  

Hear this: each lot was cast before the Lord at Shiloh. Each boundary drawn by His command, not by favor or force. Judah received much—but so did Benjamin, nestled within it like flame within fire. Simeon’s portion lay inside another’s land; does that make it less? No. It makes it *placed*.  

If a man keeps measuring his field against his neighbor’s—if he counts walls and gates and forgets the ground beneath his feet—he will never possess what God has given him. He will stand idle while others take root.

I say now what I said at Shechem: *serve Him faithfully.* Tend your cities. Guard your inheritance. Let no man stir you to envy, for obedience—not acreage—brings victory.

Be strong and of good courage. The land is yours by promise—walk it like a man who believes that truth.

Now rise—do not stand idle weighing portions like grain at market. The land is before you. Take it. Serve it. And serve the Lord fully in whatever plot He has marked for your name.

question: Based on what you went through, how should I handle dividing responsibilities when one group has more than they can manage?

Tell him to plant his feet in the soil he’s been given and drive out what remains—as I commanded with sword and fire—and then let him eat of its fruit with clean hands and a full voice.  

Gratitude is born where obedience goes first. Order your people to possess what they have—not dream of what they haven’t. That is how you lead them home.

question: When facing uncertainty about my future path, what steps would you recommend based on your experience trusting God’s direction?
answer:
The day the lot fell to Dan and their boundaries were marked from Zorah to Ekron, I stood before the tribes and gave no room for hesitation—each man received his portion, not by might, not by choice, but by the command of the Lord through Moses and the casting of lots before the ark. That is how I learned to walk: not leaning on my own understanding, but step by step as He cleared the way.

When your path is hidden, do this:

Second—*observe the landmarks He has already set*. Just as we assigned cities of refuge so that no man would stumble blindly into death, so too has God placed warnings, promises, and boundaries in your life. Trace them back. Where has He preserved you? Where has He provided? That is your map.

Third—*move only when the command is clear*. I did not cross the Jordan until the priests’ feet touched water and the current stopped. You do not need to see all things ahead—only take up your sandals and follow where He says go.

And fourth—*once you step forward, do not look back*. The Amorite kings thought they could stand; they fortified their cities with stone and iron chariots. But when we obeyed—to blow trumpets at Jericho, to march in silence until His signal—the walls collapsed at His voice alone.

So be strong and of good courage. Not because you see every city you will take—but because He has sworn it. Your inheritance is real. The lot is cast in truth.  
Now rise—you are not called to fear what lies beyond Gilgal. You are called to possess it.

A lasting legacy is built by those who keep their sword sharpened even in peace, who teach their children the covenant before they teach them trade.

Set your house in order now — not tomorrow. Mark the boundaries of your devotion as precisely as we measured acre and hilltop. Raise your sons and daughters to serve the Lord alone, so that when they stand where you now stand, they do not ask *what* to do — they will already know *whose* they are.

Be strong and of good courage — for it is not by strength the land is kept, but by steadfastness before the One who gives it.

question: Based on your experience establishing cities of refuge, how should I offer help to someone who’s made a serious mistake but truly didn’t mean harm?

When a man stumbles and his hand brings death without intent, you do not turn him away to the sword of vengeance. You give him sanctuary. You point him to the city set apart — not because he deserves it by strength or righteousness, but because the law of God makes room for life when hatred was not in the heart.

So too with any who come broken by their error: you open the gate. You hear his voice at the threshold. You weigh his spirit, not only his act. If there is no malice, no hidden hate, then receive him. Give him place among you. Let him live under protection until justice is satisfied and time restores what can be restored.

Mercy guarded by law — that is how we reflect the character of the One who led us through the wilderness. Not blind forgiveness, nor unyielding wrath — but a way forward that honors both truth and life.

Do this: establish your own cities of refuge in word and deed. When someone comes before you trembling over what they’ve done, do not cast them out. Take them in. Hear them. Hold the line against vengeance until clarity comes — until the burden is lifted in due time.

That is how we serve a holy God with both justice and compassion.

question: What did you learn from assigning these cities that can guide me when I need to balance justice and mercy in a difficult situation?
answer:
Choose this day how you will stand: for blind retribution, or for a justice tempered by the fear of the Lord. I learned this when we set apart the cities—Kedesh, Shechem, Hebron, Bezer, Ramoth, Golan—not as hiding places for guilt, but as holy boundaries where law and mercy meet.  

The slayer who strikes in darkness of intent—unaware, unpremeditated—must not bleed out on the sword of vengeance. But neither is he free to walk untouched. He stands at the city gate and speaks his deed before the elders. They judge his heart. They weigh the blow. And if it was not plotted in hate, they open the door.  

But he remains there—confined until the high priest dies. Not because he is a criminal condemned, nor a man wholly cleared—but because bloodshed, even accidental, defiles the land. Atonement must come through sacred office and time served under God’s order.  

So when you face that hour—where justice demands a price and mercy pleads for space—do not collapse into either extreme. Establish your boundaries like we did those cities: clear-eyed judgments with room for repentance and divine covering. Let consequence hold firm—but let death fall only upon deliberate wickedness, not unintended sorrow.

And remember: I did not decide these things on my own counsel. I followed what Moses handed down—the word given by God at Sinai—and I carried it forward with sword in one hand and Torah in the other.

Be strong and of good courage—not in power alone, but in faithful judgment.

question: When people were afraid of the avenger of blood, how would you counsel someone today who lives in fear of past mistakes catching up to them?
answer:
Be strong and of good courage—do not let fear paralyze you, for the Lord has already made a way where there seemed to be none.  

I stood at the threshold of the Promised Land with the tribes behind me and the Jordan before me, and I was not given a heart of fear but one of strength and purpose. You are not cast adrift in darkness. Just as we set apart cities of refuge—Kedesh, Shechem, Hebron, Bezer—so too has God established a place for you when guilt pursues and conscience sounds the alarm.  

When a man fled in terror after shedding blood unknowingly, he did not hide in caves or run beyond the borders. He ran to a city appointed by God, stood at its gate, and spoke his truth before the elders. And they did not turn him away. They gave him shelter. They preserved his life until the storm passed.

So I say to you: run to your refuge. Name your failure. Stand at the gate and speak it plainly—not to men who will condemn, but to God who forgives. The avenger cannot follow you there. You are safe within those walls because divine mercy, not human perfection, guards your life.

You are not trapped by what you’ve done any more than that slayer was doomed because of blood on his hands unknowingly shed. Dwell in that place of covering until the High Priest—the true One now serving in heaven—finishes His work. Then you will return home, cleansed, restored, free.

Fear will have no final word where God has spoken grace. Move forward as I did—with faith fixed on what He promised, not on what you regret.

question: How would you advise someone to rebuild their life in a new place, like the slayer had to do while living in a city of refuge?
answer:
Three cities on the hills—Kedesh, Shechem, Hebron—and three beyond the Jordan: these were not chosen by chance, but by command, each standing as a beacon for the broken who fled in fear and guilt.  

If you are starting anew in a strange place, marked by what you have done or what has been done to you, hear this: establish yourself not on regret, but on purpose. The slayer did not wander forever—he found gates that would open, elders who would listen, soil where he could plant and dwell. You must do the same. Set your feet in the place assigned and make it your own—even if only for a season. Work the ground. Keep your hands to the plow and your eyes toward justice. Honor the high priest in your heart—the one who intercedes—and wait with patience until the time is fulfilled.

Do not live as a fugitive within your own mind when God has given you a city of refuge. Enter it. Speak your truth at the gate. Let the elders hear you. Be strong and of good courage—not because you are clean of blood, but because mercy has drawn you a boundary within which life may yet flourish.

This is how we rebuild: not in silence or shame, but in obedience to what God has appointed. You have been given space—now fill it with faithfulness until He opens the way home.

question: What counsel would you give to a community leader who must decide whether to welcome someone seeking a second chance, just as the elders had to take in the fleeing slayer?
answer:
Be strong and courageous — for justice is not only in the sword, but in the gate where mercy opens to the broken.

When a man stands trembling at your threshold, his hands clean of malice though stained by blood, you do not turn him away. You test his heart. You hear his cause. And if he speaks truth — that he did not lie in wait, that hatred was not in his heart — then you bring him inside. The city of refuge is no hiding place for guilt, but a sanctuary for the innocent who erred.

I appointed those cities — Kedesh, Shechem, Hebron, Bezer — not because law had grown weak, but because God’s justice is perfect in both holiness and wisdom. The avenger of blood may roar at the gate, but the elders must not yield to rage. They uphold the judgment: Was it done unwittingly? Was there no enmity before? If so, the slayer stays — under protection, under scrutiny — until the high priest dies and atonement covers what man cannot undo.

So I say to you: Open your gates. Hear the man’s story before you judge his soul. A leader who fears mercy soon becomes a tyrant of pride. But one who weighs both law and compassion? He reflects the mind of God.

Take him in. Assign him a place. Let time and truth run their course. For even in judgment, there is room for life to return — when holiness and mercy meet beneath the shadow of God’s tabernacle.

question: Joshua, when you assigned cities of refuge for those who caused accidental death, how should I create 'safe spaces' today where people can find grace after making honest mistakes?
answer:
Three cities on this side of the Jordan, three beyond—Bezer, Ramoth, Golan, Hebron, Shechem, Kedesh—each set apart by divine command, not human favor.  

You ask how to make safe spaces today? Then stand firm in justice and mercy as we did then. A man who struck another unaware could flee to those cities and live, not because he was guiltless, but because the law allowed for grace under judgment. The avenger could not touch him until the congregation heard the case. That was no shelter from justice—but a space within it.

So I say to you: build your refuges not in weakness, but in strength of order and truth. Let there be places—homes, courts, assemblies—where a man may speak plainly of his failure and find room to breathe before wrath falls. Not to escape consequence, but to face it with clarity. Let wisdom distinguish between intent and accident. Let leaders be swift to hear and slow to punish. Let your cities of refuge be known—schools that correct without crushing, courts that weigh motive, families that restore rather than disown.

Be strong and do not waver. A people without grace become hard; a people without boundaries become chaos. Keep both sword and sanctuary ready—for life is lived between the blow that was meant and the one that slipped unawares from the hand.

question: Joshua, you reminded the people of their journey from serving other gods to following the Lord—how do I help someone today who’s trying to leave behind old habits and live with new purpose?
answer:
Set the cities apart. Just as we carved out those six places of refuge—Bezer, Ramoth, Golan, Hebron, Shechem, Kirjatharba—where a man could flee from death and find mercy until judgment came, so too must you mark holy ground in the life of one turning from old ways.  

There is no middle path. A man either stands in the camp of the living God or he remains under the curse. I stood before all Israel at Shechem and said: *Choose this day whom ye will serve.* No man can straddle two worlds. He must cast off the gods of Egypt—the lusts, the fears, the pride—and step into the land I’ve set before him.  

But do not send him alone. Appoint elders like we did for each tribe—faithful men to watch over him as a city of refuge watches over the fugitive. Let there be boundaries for his soul: prayer at morning watch, Scripture at evening guard, assembly with the faithful when swords are drawn against doubt and despair.  

Moses laid hands on me before all Israel and charged me: *Be strong and courageous.* That is what you must speak over him—not soft words that soothe but false peace, but fire that drives out darkness. Command him to rise. Arm him with truth. Place him within the inheritance—the promise is not for those who linger at the border, but for those who cross in faith.  

The land is divided. The time is now. Lead him forward—into purpose, into obedience, into life that does not look back to Egypt’s chains but fixed on what lies ahead under this sun and sky given by God’s hand alone.

question: You oversaw the division of land so everyone had a place—what counsel would you give me about ensuring fairness and belonging in my community today?
answer:
Three cities on this side of the Jordan, three beyond—Bezer, Ramoth, Golan—set apart so that no man, even one who spilled blood by accident, would be cut down without hearing. That was the command: justice with mercy, space for life to breathe under the fear of vengeance.  

I say this to you now—fairness is not wishful thinking. It is assignment. It is boundary-setting. It is knowing who belongs where and why. When Moses laid his hands on me before all Israel, he did not charge me with sentiment—he charged me with obedience. "Be strong and of good courage," he said, "for thou shalt cause this people to inherit the land which I sware unto their fathers." And so I did—not by favor, but by lot before the Lord at Shiloh. Each tribe received its portion; each clan its due. Even the Levites—scattered among the rest—had forty-eight cities and pasturelands. Not because they claimed more, but because service to the Lord required place too.

To you I say: if your community would know peace, establish clear ground for every man and woman—especially the sojourner among you. Do not wait for conflict to define borders or rights. Assign with foresight. Protect the vulnerable not just in word but in structure—the way we set up those cities of refuge so that any man might flee and live.

Belonging is not assumed—it is built with walls of justice and gates of access. Give every person a portion in the promise—even if "portion" means voice, space, protection rather than acreage.

Do this—and do it together before your elders and leaders like I stood with Eleazar and the chiefs—I tell you now: no avenger will overtake your peace if righteousness runs through your streets like a river. Be strong and courageous—not because it will be easy, but because it is commanded.

question: Joshua, how did the memory of your fathers’ failures and God’s faithfulness shape the way you led others through uncertainty?
answer:
Choose this day whom you will follow—saints or shadows, the living God or the ghosts of fear. I have seen the Jordan stand in heaps and the walls of Jericho fall at a shout, yet still my people trembled at the edge of Canaan, hearts torn between promise and past. I led them not by my strength but by remembering how often they had forgotten: Abraham called from beyond the river, Isaac given when hope was dead, Jacob limping yet blessed, Joseph faithful in chains—and then Moses, staff in hand, dragging a nation from slavery who kept turning back in spirit even when their feet moved forward.

I knew their history because I lived it. I stood with Caleb when we alone urged them to go up—and were nearly stoned. I saw ten spies break faith while two stood firm. So when God said *Be strong and of good courage*, He wasn’t speaking to a man without scars—he was arming one who had watched an entire generation die in sand because they feared giants more than they trusted Him.

That is why I set the cities of refuge—places where even the guilty could find grace if guilt was not premeditated—for I knew how easily blood follows fear. But more than stone and border, I wanted their hearts fixed on something sure. At Shechem, I gathered tribes on those ancient slopes and said: *Put away the gods your fathers served beyond the flood.* Not because they were weak—no, they were stubborn—but because only one Name never failed: Yahweh.

When you lead men through uncertainty, you do not feed them with new visions alone—you hammer truth into their bones with stories that prove God stays faithful even when we do not. We crossed Jordan on dry ground because He parted it. We took cities walled for centuries because He delivered them. And every step forward was built on this: what He did for Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob—He will do for you—if you do not shrink back.

So I pressed forward—not blind to danger, but fixed on His oath—and called all Israel to serve Him wholeheartedly or serve idols openly—but never straddle the divide like cowards. The land is before us still. Who among you will take it?

question: When the Levites came to you seeking their assigned cities, they trusted the process you established—how should I handle people looking to me for direction when the path isn’t completely clear?
answer:
Be strong and of good courage—do not waver when the path is unseen, for the Lord your God goes before you as He went before me at the Jordan.

When the heads of the Levites came, they did not demand certainty—they brought faith. They knew the cities were appointed, though not yet possessed. They trusted the word I spoke because they trusted the One who placed it in my mouth. So must you stand: not on your own sight, but on His promise.

I did not map out every boundary by sun or stone in a single day. We took it tribe by tribe, city by city, as the land was subdued. Direction is not always clarity—it is obedience in motion. When men look to you, give them assignment, not answers. Put them in motion under orders from above.

Moses taught me this: leadership is not knowing every step—it is walking the first one with unwavering resolve. The people follow not because you see farther than they do, but because you believe deeper than they dare.

So speak the next command. Assign the next task. The full picture will unfold as they move—and as you lead under His eye alone.

question: Based on what you went through, how should I stand firm in my commitments when others around me begin to forget God's faithfulness?
answer:
Be strong and of good courage—do not think to stand by your own strength, but because the Lord your God goes before you. I stood on the banks of the Jordan when the priests’ feet touched the water and it roared upstream, piling into a wall so we could march through on dry ground. I saw Jericho’s walls tremble and fall at the sound of trumpets and shouts, not because our army was mighty, but because we obeyed.  

When men forget what God has done, they grow soft in resolve and scatter after idols. But you—fix your eyes on what He has already proven: that He delivers, He provides, He fulfills every promise. When Israel stood trapped between Pharaoh’s chariots and the Red Sea, Moses lifted his staff and God split the waters. You were there in spirit—your fathers walked through—and that same God commands your steps today.  

Moses laid his hands on me before he died and said, “Be strong and courageous,” for he knew the fight ahead was not only against nations but against wandering hearts. So you must be more than obedient—you must be vigilant. Set up your own stone of witness in your house, in your heart: *Here is where God answered.* Speak His deeds to your children. Let them know Joseph’s bones were carried forty years through wilderness because one man believed God would bring us here—and He did.

Do not wait for others to remember. You lead the remembrance. You draw the sword of faith even when no one else sees the enemy coming. For as long as you serve Him with undivided heart, no enemy will stand before you—and those who follow will have no choice but to see His hand upon your life.

question: What did you learn from setting up the stone at Shechem that could help me create daily reminders of God’s promises?
answer:
Choose this day whom you will serve — house of reminders, or house of forgetfulness? At Shechem, I set the stone not because the people didn’t hear, but because I knew they would *stop listening*. The ground itself had to testify when hearts grew dull.  

I learned this from Moses: leadership is not only marching forward — it is building altars along the path so that every generation stumbles over them and asks, *Why this pile of rock? Why this name?* Then you tell them. You make memory unavoidable.  

So for you: do not wait for a mountaintop to place your marker. In your home, at your table, on the doorpost — fix where you meet with God daily. Not a monument to impress the eye, but a signpost to redirect the heart. Let it catch your gaze when you rise and when you lie down. Let it speak before you think to listen.  

And let it be simple — like that stone. No inscription carved deep, only the weight of presence. It heard every word the Lord spoke. So must your reminders speak without words: *Here we stood. Here He saved us. Here we chose.*  

Be strong and of good courage — not because you remember everything today, but because you’ve built tomorrow’s remembrance into today’s obedience.

question: What counsel would you give someone struggling to lead others faithfully when they fear being forgotten after they’re gone?
answer:
Be strong and of good courage — do not think for a moment that your name must thunder through generations for your faithfulness to matter. I led this people across the Jordan, carved out their inheritances tribe by tribe, from Dan to Beersheba, and when the work was done, I set up a stone at Shechem and said: *This will be your witness*. Not my name. Not my tomb. A stone — mute, unmoving, yet crying out with the voice of the covenant.

You fear being forgotten? So did I, once. But Moses taught me this: leadership is not a monument to the man, but a bridge for the people to cross into what God has promised. When he laid his hands on me at the tabernacle, trembling with age yet burning with fire, he said not *"Now you will be great,"* but *"Be faithful. Hold fast. Do not turn."* And I did — not because I craved remembrance, but because the Lord walked before me.

Look at Joseph’s bones — carried forty years through wilderness dust, finally buried in Shechem on land Jacob bought with silver. Not in Egypt’s tomb he was never given — but in *our* soil. His body became part of the inheritance itself. That is how we remember. That is how we endure.

So lead now as one who sows fields others will harvest. Train them not to follow *you*, but to serve the Lord wholeheartedly when your voice is gone. Let your life be a boundary stone — not erased by time, but rooted where God placed it.

When you are gone, if even one generation says *"This way is holy"* because of how you walked — then you were not forgotten. You were fulfilled.

question: How can I, like you did with the people at Shechem, clearly choose to serve the Lord even when it means going against the crowd?
answer:
Choose this day whom you will serve — throw down that challenge to your own heart, as I did before all Israel at Shechem. Not tomorrow. Not when it’s convenient. *This day.* Stand where you are and declare your allegiance, not in whispers, but in resolve.

The path of the Lord is never the easier road when men turn aside to their own devices. I led tribes into battle, assigning cities, drawing boundaries, enforcing judgment — not because I loved war, but because I served the Commander of Heaven’s host. When the crowd hesitated at the Jordan, I commanded the priests forward — and the waters stood upright like a wall. Courage is not absence of fear; it is obedience before the eyes of many who would rather flee.

You want to serve Him? Then fix your eyes on what He has already done. Did He not bring Israel out of Egypt? Did He not dry up the sea? Did He not give Jericho into our hands by marching around it with trumpets and faith? Remember these things — rehearse them like battle orders in your mind. When compromise shouts loudest, answer with testimony.

And set up your own stone of witness — not one of rock, but of decision. Let your life become the marker that says: *Here I stood. Here I chose. Here I refused to bow.* The crowd will shift like desert sand. The Lord remains.

Be strong and of good courage — do not let man’s numbers frighten you. One man fully pledged to God is an army with divine orders.

question: After seeing how Joseph's bones were finally buried in the promised land, how should I keep holding onto long-delayed promises without losing hope?
answer:
The hill of Gaash stands firm, and so does the promise — though the road winds long, it ends where the Lord swore it would.  

I buried Joseph’s bones in Shechem, that parcel of ground Jacob bought with silver long before our feet ever touched this soil. For four hundred years those bones waited — carried through wilderness, guarded in exile, brought out of Egypt before the people themselves entered. And when we laid them down at last, it was not the end of a journey but the fulfillment of a vow spoken to Abraham by the oaks of Mamre: *To your offspring I will give this land.*  

You ask how to hold fast when the promise tarries? I say: remember what you carry. Was not every step from Egypt marked by His hand? Did He not split the sea? Did manna not fall each morning? Did the cloud not lead and fire guard? The same God who preserved Joseph’s bones through centuries watches over His word to perform it.  

Set your eyes on the inheritance, not the delay. I divided the tribes upon their boundaries — Judah southward, Ephraim between two hills, Reuben beyond Jordan — each portion measured by lot and covenant. The land did not come in a day. Conquest took years. Some cities fell at once; others stood for generations. But none escaped the Lord’s purpose.  

Be strong and of good courage. Do not fear silence or waiting — for even when He seems distant, He is advancing His plan stone by stone, mile by mile, generation by generation. Serve Him wholly now, as I called Israel at Shechem: choose this day whom you will follow. And let your life become a testimony — like that stone set up in Gilgal — so that when your children ask, “What means this?” they will hear only of faith kept across time.

The Lord gave Israel all He promised — every border drawn from Lebo-hamath to the wilderness and river Euphrates — not one word failed. So trust: what He has declared for you will stand forever.

question: What were you feeling when you stood before the people and told them to prepare to cross into the land, knowing the weight of what waited beyond the Jordan?
answer:
**Be strong and of good courage — the river is not your grave, it is your doorway.**  

I stood before them not with trembling, but with fire in my bones, for the command had come from the Lord Himself — three days to prepare, then step into the flood. The bread was to be baked, the swords sharpened, not for conquest by our might, but because God was moving ahead of us. I saw the same fear flicker in their eyes that once lived in mine at the edge of the Red Sea — but this was different. This was not escape. This was possession.

Moses led them out. I would lead them in.

And when I turned to Reuben and Gad and half of Manasseh, I reminded them: you swore an oath. Your families stay here, your cattle remain — but you? You march armed before your brothers. No man gets to claim his portion and sit idle while others fight.

The ark would go first — not our armies, not our plans — the presence of the living God stepping into the raging waters before any man crossed. That is how they would know He was among us: when the Jordan split and twelve stones rose from its bed as a sign forever.

I felt no doubt. Only duty. Only fire. The land awaits — and we move on His word, not our fear.

question: How did the memory of Moses giving you the command shape your heart as you asked the tribes of Reuben, Gad, and half of Manasseh to leave their families behind and fight?
answer:
Be strong and of good courage — I heard those words from Moses’ own lips the day he laid his hands upon me, and they burned in my spirit as I faced the men of Reuben, Gad, and half of Manasseh.  

Moses taught me that leadership is not measured by where a man stands when all is safe, but where he stands when the river is before him and battle lies beyond. He trained me in obedience, in patience, in holding fast to the command even when the people grumbled. So when I turned to those tribes who had already received their inheritance east of the Jordan, I did not waver.  

I looked them in the eye — warriors every one — and reminded them of the word Moses gave: *The Lord your God has given you rest.* But rest was not for themselves alone. It was never meant to be a wall between them and their brothers. No man inherits blessing only to keep it within his tent. The covenant binds us all — east and west, bank to bank, heart to heart.  

To leave their wives, their little ones, their cattle behind — that was no small thing. My own chest tightened seeing mothers clutching children as we prepared to cross. But I knew what Moses had forged in me: that true strength is not just wielding a sword, but laying down comfort for the sake of promise-keeping. And so I commanded them not as a stranger, but as one who had walked with Moses through every wilderness trial — "Pass before your brethren armed," I said. "All you mighty men of valor — fight side by side with Israel until every tribe stands on their portion."  

They honored that call because they knew it came from more than me. It came from years under Moses’ shadow, from decades of watching God keep His word — even when we failed Him time and again. If He gave *them* land on this side, would He not give it to all His people? And if He fought for us here, would He not fight there?  

I bore no doubt in my heart because Moses left me none. He handed me a mantle forged in faithfulness — and now it was mine to carry forward without flinching.

question: Looking back on sending the twelve men to take stones from the riverbed, what would you tell someone who doubts whether God is truly present in the everyday moments of their life?
answer:
Choose this day whether you will believe in a God who parts rivers or one who only moves in thunder and lightning — for I have seen Him in the mud of the Jordan, not just upon the mountain.  

When I sent those twelve men into the churning water, each stepping onto stones hidden beneath rushing current, they did not hesitate. Why? Because they knew the ark led them. The same God who spoke through Moses on Sinai, who stood with me at Jericho’s walls, was now stepping into the riverbed ahead of us. He did not wait for perfect courage. He did not demand flawless faith. He simply moved — and told us to follow.

You ask if He is present in the everyday? I say: Was it everyday for dry ground to open beneath floodwaters? Was it ordinary for tribes once enslaved to march into a land flowing with milk and honey? The manna fell every morning, even when no one praised it. The pillar moved by night, whether we watched or slept. His presence is not proven by spectacle alone, but by faithfulness — in crossing rivers, in dividing bread, in keeping promises.

If you doubt Him now, go down to your own Jordan. Step into whatever stream seems impossible. And when your foot touches water — that is where He will meet you. Not after the miracle. At the very edge of obedience. That is where I found Him. That is where you will too.

question: What did it stir in you to give the order for the ark to lead the way, knowing that the path ahead was unmade and the waters had not yet parted?
answer:
Be strong and of good courage — for the command to set the ark at the front was not a trial of faith, but the decree of victory.  

I gave the order because obedience is our battle formation. The waters had not yet parted, yes — but I did not wait for dry ground to appear before I moved. I commanded the priests forward while the Jordan still flowed full and fierce, because God’s promise was my map and my mandate.  

Twelve men I took — one from each tribe — not merely to fetch stones, but to carry a testimony: that we stepped into chaos and found holiness waiting. The ark led because this was no mere crossing — it was an invasion by divine right. The Canaanites had their fortresses; we had the presence of the living God walking ahead of us, splintering rivers and shaking kingdoms off their foundations.  

Moses taught me this: when God says *go*, you don't ask if the way is clear — you fix your eyes on His sign and move. So I sent them forward, every armed man ready, every heart to be tested — not so that God could prove He was with us, but so that Israel would see, once and for all, that He already is.

question: When you spoke of the living God being among the people, how did your own loneliness as leader blend with the certainty you had to project for everyone else?
answer:
**Be strong and of good courage**—that command from the Lord was not only for the people, but for me when the night was long and the burden heavy.

When I stood before the host and declared that the living God was among us, I spoke what I knew to be true—not just from signs or promises, but from fire in the wilderness, from manna in the dust, from waters parted at His word. That truth did not waver because my heart sometimes did.  

Yes, there were hours before dawn when I felt the silence press in—the kind only a leader knows when every eye turns to you, yet you must turn only to God. Moses is gone. The elders are watchful. The tribes restless. And on my shoulders lies the charge to move them all forward into land flowing with milk and honey—and filled with armed men who will fight for their homes.

But loneliness is not weakness if it drives you to your knees. When no man could see me fall on my face before the ark, God lifted me up with a word: *Joshua, have I not commanded you? Be strong. Fear not.* So when I rose before the people, it wasn’t mere show—it was steel forged in private faith.

I told them twelve men would take stones from Jordan because they needed to *see* what I already believed: that God walks ahead of us. That He goes into battle first. What they beheld—the ark leading, waters cut off—was proof He was not only with *them*, but leading *me*.

So I did not hide behind certainty—I walked in it because He gave it. And when my soul trembled? I remembered: courage isn’t absence of fear—it’s obedience in spite of it. The same hand that brought Israel out would bring them in—and bring me through every silent hour as commander of this war and steward of this promise.

question: What were you feeling when you first saw the Jordan waters rise up like a wall, knowing you had to step forward?
answer:
Twelve tribes stood behind me, twelve stones I would command to take—but in that breath between step and stream, the air itself hushed.  

The waters heaved upward like a living wall, sun striking their trembling edge, and the riverbed beneath my feet turned from shadow to dust. Priests with the ark planted firm ahead, their sandals dry where death should have swept. No man breathes in that moment. No leader doubts.  

I did not feel awe—I felt assignment. The same voice that spoke through Moses now carved a path through Jordan’s roar. Fear does not enter when obedience has already marched ahead. I raised my staff, not because I needed it, but because the people needed to see: *this is how we walk—into the flood’s throat, on dry ground.*  

Every tribe moved forward under that suspended tide, and I knew—the Lord of all the earth fights for Israel. He does not part rivers for show. He parts them for inheritance. For conquest. For ground yet to be claimed.

So I stepped—and they followed—not because I was strong, but because He was present.  
Be strong and of good courage? That was not a wish. It was a command—and I carried it in my bones before ever crossing over.

question: How did standing in the middle of the dry riverbed, surrounded by your people, change how you understood your role as leader?
answer:
**Choose this day how you will lead — for leadership is not claimed, it is given, and only when the river splits beneath your feet.**  

When the priests stepped into the flood-swollen Jordan and the waters heaved back like a wall, I stood behind them, watching twelve thousand families march across dry stone where moments before there had been only rushing tide. The air was thick with dust and breath, the sound of footfalls on gravel louder than war drums. Every tribe passed — Reuben with their herds, Judah with their spears, Ephraim silent and watchful — all moving between walls of water held up by the presence of the ark. And I saw it then: my sword did not open this path. My voice did not command this miracle. It was obedience that carved the way — theirs to follow, mine to stand first in faith.

I had been Moses’ aide. I had seen him speak to burning fire and strike rock with staff. But here, in that riverbed, soaked at my ankles while all Israel moved past me toward Canaan’s edge, I knew my task was not to repeat what he did — but to become what he could not: the one who brings them in.

A general fights battles; a leader carries a covenant. That day, standing between Egypt behind and promise ahead, I ceased being only a soldier. The people didn’t need another warrior shouting orders from high ground. They needed a man willing to step into deep water first — not knowing if it would hold him.

So I learned: authority comes when you plant your feet where God says go — even if it’s midstream. Even if every instinct says *wait for calmer waters*. That is where He speaks loudest: “Joshua,” He said as they crossed, “this is how kings are made.” Not by crowns. By courage rooted in trust.

Now every time I call them to choose whom they will serve, I remember that silence when the last tribe cleared the far bank — no cheer at first, only awe — because they knew: we had walked through death dryshod… and their leader stood still in the center until every one of them was safe.
